I never normally book a Ryanair flight, but for my first trip abroad in a year following surgery, I succumbed to a package deal with TUI (because I had been gifted a voucher) which included a Ryanair flight to Porto. I cringed at myself, because I just had this feeling that the usual Ryanair troubles would befall me. I was fully aware that Ryanair had moved to an online only boarding pass system through their app, as it had been widely reported. I made sure to download their app and tried to download my boarding pass on the Tuesday before my flight (Sunday). I could only do so by paying extra to get my booking fee in advance, so I waited until the Saturday to get my boarding pass in the free-of-charge period 24 hours before flying.The app didn't work, but I didn't know that because there was no way of verifying whether I had my boarding pass or not. The app took me into a doom loop; I was unable to go forward because the app said I had already completed the action, but I could not see the boarding pass and there was no indication that the app had failed.When I reached Edinburgh airport I could not check in with the app and was directed to a Customer Service desk (not Ryanair staff). I could not do anything except pay £55 to have them print a paper boarding pass. I immediately filed a complaint before I even boarded the plane.Many weeks passed. Nothing happened. I reminded them this week that I was not going away and re-stated my case. I got an automated reply saying I should try a different browser, even though the fault was with Ryanair's own app, not a browser webpage. My case was closed. I continued writing to them. They sent more AI slop. I asked a human to get involved. My case was closed again with no comment.Ryanair have clearly created a situation where the failure of their process leaves passengers unable to do anything except pay to get out of the mess they have initiated. I think they may have factored this into their business model; now and then, a failure in the app will force a customer to pay £55 to get a printed boarding pass when there is no other way to proceed. I am sure others are being exploited in this way and I simply cannot live with the idea that Ryanair can get away with this. I am committed to make them refund me, and I will do everything I can to achieve that. But I would also like others to know about this trap which Ryanair have engineered into their process and which leaves passengers paying for their faults or unable to fly.UPDATE: I have looked over reviews here on Trustpilot for the last seven days. Of the reviews left for Ryanair, 13 (including mine) explain the same situation - forced to pay a £55 fee for a boarding pass because the app did not work. If THIRTEEN people are coming here to complain, imagine how many other people are being ripped off by Ryanair - including people who don't complain or people who blamed themselves. This is a scandal.

Seriously, vote with your feet. They ALWAYS disappoint in every single area ! AVOID, AVOID, AVOID. I WILL NEVER FLY WITH RYAN AIR AGAIN.

When I boarded, the seats next to me were completely empty, yet I was told he could not sit next to me due to “weight distribution.” This explanation made little sense, especially as passengers are not weighed before boarding. During the delay, my boyfriend did sit next to me and was not asked to move once the flight went ahead, which strongly suggests the original explanation was not truthful.I understand that Ryanair is a budget airline, but the level of greed around seating and hidden costs is excessive. More importantly, the lack of basic care, communication, and customer service during a long on-board delay was unacceptable. Passengers were not looking for freebies — just a basic level of care and honesty as paying customers.

You probably pay emotionally too.But the peak absurdity was the check-in situation. I tried multiple times to check in online and it simply did NOT work. Not my fault, not my device, not my internet - just Ryanair being Ryanair.So I arrive at the airport for my flight from Athens to London (a route where I understand they may need to verify visas). Fine. Check my documents. No problem.Except… they charged me €55 to check in at the airport.€55.For something I tried - and was unable - to do online.Let’s be clear: I wasn’t lazy. I wasn’t late. I wasn’t careless. I was forced into airport check-in by their broken system and then punished for it.Charging passengers for your own technical failure is not “low-cost airline strategy.” It’s a scam with wings.I got on the chat with the company and it was mentioned that check in and verification of documents are different things. I needed to the check in online so they could verify my documents. Again, how am I doing my check in online if I was enable to???Old planes, zero service, constant upselling, and policies designed to trap you rather than help you. If this airline spent half as much effort on customer experience as it does on extracting fees, it might actually be tolerable.Cheap ticket? Sure.Dignity? Gone.Never flying Ryanair again and I strongly recommend anyone who values basic fairness to do the same.⭐️ 0/5 if that were possible.

I cannot verify Ryanair’s internal procedures, but I can state what happened to me as a paying passenger on Flight FR1679 (Birmingham → Corfu, 08 May 2025).I had already purchased Priority & 2 Cabin Bags, clearly shown on my booking confirmation. ryan air email 1Despite this, I was stopped at the boarding gate and charged £75 for a cabin bag that was allegedly “one inch oversized.” I witnessed multiple passengers with equal or larger cabin bags walk through unchecked. That inconsistency is factual and forms the basis of my complaint.At the gate:Staff member Mitchell insisted the £75 payment was “policy,” but did not explain why policy was enforced only against me.His colleague Mollie refused to issue a VAT receipt, handing me instead a handwritten yellow luggage tag (Manual Bag Tag FR422334) which is not a VAT-compliant receipt. Formal ComplaintShe refused to show ID while working in a public-facing role.I was told a receipt would be emailed. No receipt ever arrived.On the return flight from Corfu to Birmingham, the same bag was accepted without any issue. This demonstrates that the original enforcement was inconsistent.This happened on my 20th wedding anniversary, adding unnecessary stress and embarrassment.❌ Ryanair’s Complaint Handling Breaches Basic Consumer ProtectionsI submitted a formal written complaint through Ryanair’s portal with full evidence.The complaint was closed without addressing any of the issues raised. My request for a human response is visible in the case file. A_Practical_Treatise_on_Bills_o…Instead of providing a resolution, Ryanair issued a generic automated reply that ignored every specific point of my complaint.Based on the Consumer Rights Act 2015:✔ Section 49 – Reasonable Care & SkillA service must be carried out with reasonable care and skill.Inconsistent enforcement of baggage rulesFailure to issue a legally compliant receiptRefusal to provide staff identificationThese actions raise clear concerns under this section.✔ Section 50 – Information Provided to the Consumer Is BindingIf a consumer pays for “Priority & 2 Cabin Bags,” the airline must honour that service as described. My booking shows I paid for that entitlement. ryan air email 1Charging me again at the gate contradicts the service paid for.✔ Section 52 – Service Must Be Performed Within a Reasonable TimeClosing my complaint without addressing it, and providing no meaningful response, fails even the minimum standard of timely and fair handling.✔ VAT RegulationsA £75 charge is a commercial transaction. Issuing an informal handwritten tag instead of a proper receipt does not meet UK requirements for VAT-compliant documentation.I requested a proper receipt and did not receive one.❌ Summary of FailuresBased solely on documented facts:Unfair and inconsistent application of baggage policy£75 charge applied without justificationNo VAT-compliant receipt providedStaff refused IDComplaint closed without any investigation or human responseReturn flight accepted the same bag with no issuesEmotional distress caused during a special anniversary tripThis was one of the poorest consumer experiences I’ve had with any airline.⭐ What I Requested (Still Unresolved):Refund of the £75 unfair chargeVAT-compliant receiptExplanation of inconsistent baggage enforcementA human, not automated, responseAccountability for staff behaviour at the boarding gateRyanair has provided none of the above.Consumers deserve fairness, transparency, and proper complaint handling.Unfortunately, this experience did not meet even the minimum standards required under UK consumer law.

As a seasoned business traveler who flys ~80 flights per year I cringe when there is no alternative but to fly Ryan air. This airline has strategically designed their structure to con as many passengers as possible out of their money. For example, when you ad a check-in bag, every airline in the world starts at 23kg allowance, business class is often 32kg, but Ryan air only give you 10kg, and they hide that as much as they can… You have to be aware and experienced enough as a traveller to know 10kg is never going to be enough and then you get to the airport you’ll be charged $70-100 even if you’re just few Kilos over. It’s a con, designed to trap the inexperienced traveller by making their system unlike any others airline with tricks and games and limited information to try and catch you out. Take my advice, pay a few extra dollars and fly with a more transparent and genuine company. It’s cheaper and far less stressful in the long run. Ryan Air should be held accountable and in an ideal world, shut down for its predatory business practices.

There was no follow up from the airline and I was only able to call to obtain a link for chatting to follow up.  They kept saying they were processing cases that were about 3 months old. On 5 December 2025, I made a car rental booking at Birmingham Airport, UK. About 45 minutes after receiving the confirmation, due to an unexpected issue, I had to cancel the booking. I contacted their official customer service immediately, the cancellation was confirmed, and I was given a case reference (Case Ref: 03045653).Initially, my refund was refused on the grounds that I had supposedly not requested the cancellation within the allowed timeframe, despite the fact that I provided clear evidence (call logs, timestamps, confirmations). After this argument was disproven, the company looked for another reason to refuse the refund.They then claimed that my driving licence had not been held for 2 years, basing this solely on the issue date of my renewed licence, while completely ignoring the fact that I have held a driving licence for over 20 years. This was a purely administrative renewal, not a newly obtained licence, which is clearly shown on the document.In the end, the case was closed unilaterally based on this unreasonable interpretation, without properly considering the explanations or the documents provided.I have flown with Ryanair many times over the years, but after this experience, I will avoid using and paying for their additional services as much as possible.I am writing this review to warn other customers to be extremely careful when booking services through Ryanair. In my case, it felt very clear that different reasons were used successively so that I would lose the money, while the company would keep it.Unfortunately, a very unfair and frustrating experience.Booking Ref: GB889711690  Case Ref: 03041907

I rarely write reviews, but my recent experience with Ryanair’s customer service was genuinely shocking.I needed help with a simple situation: I am leaving Malta permanently and discovered I cannot take my cat on my booked Ryanair flight due to their pet policy. I hadn’t planned to bring my cat, but when re-homing fell through, I had no choice. This meant cancelling my flight and rebooking with another airline, making all the extra luggage I had purchased completely unnecessary.I contacted Ryanair to ask about cancelling or at least receiving a goodwill refund for the unused luggage add-ons. My questions were dismissed, no solution was offered, and I was repeatedly referred to the terms and conditions. When I tried to clarify my options, chats were closed without any attempt to resolve the issue.When I tried to escalate via their internal complaint system (the only available option), things became worse. Every complaint I submitted was automatically closed. I kept receiving generic replies asking for “more information” despite having provided everything. Live Chat looped me back to the chatbot with no way to reach a human, and email support simply redirected me back to the same broken portal. No one ever acknowledged or addressed the actual content of my complaint.Ryanair effectively made it impossible to communicate with a human being. It genuinely feels like the system is designed to frustrate customers until they give up. I understand policies, but a functioning complaints process is a basic consumer right—especially in the EU—and the level of obstruction I experienced was unlike anything I’ve seen from a major airline.I wasn’t asking for anything unreasonable, just a fair conversation and some goodwill for costs I could no longer use due to circumstances outside my control. Instead, I was met with automated loops, closed cases, and dismissive handling. This experience alone is enough for me to avoid Ryanair in the future.

Avoid at all costs! Our Poznan–Edinburgh flight (9th February 2026) was handled extremely poorly from start to finish.We were first kept on the ground in Poznan for almost 2 hours with no proper explanation. At one point it was suggested the delay related to a disabled passenger, which created confusion rather than clarity. There was no transparent communication. What is particularly concerning is that Edinburgh Airport’s midnight closure had already caused a diversion just two weeks earlier in January. Despite this known issue, there was no warning that diversion was even a possibility. We were not given the opportunity to notify family or arrange backup transport. We only learned we were being diverted approximately 10 minutes before landing when the pilot announced it. All communication was delivered in English only, despite the aircraft being full of Polish passengers, including many elderly people who clearly did not understand what was happening. We landed in Glasgow Prestwick at around 01:00am. Cabin crew had announced that coaches would be provided. No coaches were available. One bus eventually arrived and appeared to take crew and a small number of passengers, leaving the majority of us stranded at a remote airport with no open shops, no water, and no assistance.There was no public transport operating at that time (first services around 5–6am). I was travelling with my 8-year-old child. Remaining overnight in an empty airport was not a safe or reasonable option. Ryanair sent a message instructing passengers to source their own transport and keep receipts for reimbursement. My partner had to drive from Edinburgh to collect us. I submitted the petrol receipt exactly as instructed. The claim was rejected on the basis that petrol is “not eligible”, despite there being no buses, trains or coaches available. The customer service experience afterwards was just as disappointing. I received only generic template responses that did not address the actual circumstances of the case. Each reply simply directed me to passenger rights information on their website. No specific explanation, no meaningful review, and no resolution whatsoever. When I asked for clarification, I was effectively blocked from further escalation. The lack of accountability and the repeated use of automated responses made an already stressful situation even worse. Passengers should be aware that even if you are told to arrange your own transport and keep receipts, reimbursement may still be refused without proper consideration of the circumstances. This was an extremely stressful experience for me and my child, and the handling of it afterwards has been equally disappointing.

If I could rate Ryanair below zero, I would. I booked this flight solely because it was the cheapest and fastest option for what should have been a straightforward 30-minute trip from Barcelona to Palma de Mallorca. Instead, it turned into a complete nightmare.I arrived at the airport early for my scheduled 9:00 PM Sunday departure. The flight was first delayed due to thunderstorms and pushed to 10:00 PM. Oddly enough, flights to Ibiza and Formentera continued departing without issue, while ours was delayed again—this time to 11:00 PM and no rain! At 11:00 PM, the flight was cancelled outright.Passengers were then forced to wait in a two-hour line to speak with customer service. The staff appeared completely unprepared and uninterested in assisting anyone. Eye-rolling, dismissive attitudes, and zero problem-solving skills were on full display. I work in customer service myself—if you don’t know an answer, you find one. That did not happen here.The only option offered was rebooking onto the next night’s 9:00 PM flight at no cost to us, which, unsurprisingly, was also delayed until nearly 11:00 PM. We were told we would be fully compensated, at least for our hotel accommodations. I had to spend $188 on a last-minute hotel after arriving back in Barcelona at 1:00 AM, grateful just to get some rest.Attempting to receive reimbursement from Ryanair was another ordeal entirely. Their website would not allow me to complete the refund form because it requires an IBAN number—something that does not exist for U.S. bank cards. From September until December 16th, I went back and forth trying to resolve this, only to receive a partial refund of $120, not the full amount owed.I fully understand that this is a budget airline and that expectations should be adjusted accordingly—but this level of disorganization, poor customer service, and refusal to properly reimburse expenses is unacceptable. Ridiculous.

Decided to go to Benidorm with family for a few days over new year, usually don’t use Ryanair but flights were booked by family and not myself. Flight to Benidorm not too bad nothing major happened however, returning to the uk was a horrible experience. Only just arrived at the airport to check in bags, mind you, me and my mother both have disabilities (me having mental health difficulties, and my mum more physical) so we were at the accessible check in desk. When checking 2 bags in, we were certain 2 bags had been payed for as a 20kg cabin add on, however lady at the desk claimed we only payed for 1, and no others showing up. I looked through the app (we had two separate bookings for the four of us) and should have been 1 bag under each booking. I did notice there wasn’t a bag in one of the bookings however after a thorough look there was two bags under 1 of the bookings so regardless it should have shown in Ryanair’s system. She swore blind that we only bought one bag and was quick to ask us to purchase an add on. At the time (before checking the app, yes I should have checked before but so much going on we didn’t get round to it prior to arriving) so we agreed to buy an extra cabin bag as we thought we must have forgot to add one on originally. After this, I usually get assistance through security so we waited for that on the accessible seating. Whilst waiting I looked through the app and discovered what I had mentioned previously about two bags under one booking. We bought this to the lady’s attention, had a quick skim through her system and still was adamant that only one bag had been payed for and what we were seeing on the app isn’t on her system and so therefore we must be incorrect according to her. To be honest the whole interaction was quite rude and quick to push the blame on us, when it’s very clear it’s a mistake on their system - or she wasn’t looking properly at the bookings. as we had now understood we payed for three bags when we only wanted 2, we asked to be reimbursed for what we shouldn’t have payed for, yet funnily enough she was unable to do that - strange they’re quick to take your money though. So she sent us to help desk who managed to sort a refund after 20 minutes of waiting around. He then told us we had to go back and change the stickers on passport as the bag we checked in would have changed. We went back to the desk where the lady was checking bags in, and sorted the stickers, when she then finally admitted to making a mistake claiming it was her first day on the job. Now I understand being new at a job and you can make mistakes, I’m new at a waitress job myself so I get it, but when they aren’t willing to get a co worker or anyone to assist and instead make us run around like idiots to fix THEIR mistake, is just unacceptable. This was exhausting and extremely frustrating as they were very aware of our struggles (sunflower lanyards, as well as hidden disability displayed on the bookings) (mind you she didn’t even seem to realise my mum also should have had assistance but we didn’t have time to clear that up and frankly nor did we have the energy to fight that). It’s just a horrible situation to be in for anyone, let alone with anxiety etc, I could see my mum getting extremely anxious to the point she was shaking. The time it took to resolve this left us with less than 15 minutes to grab something to eat and board. All in all I can say I have very little faith in the support at Ryanair, and won’t be using them in the future. Hope this helps anyone get an insight so they’re aware next time they choose to travel.

I would like  to raise a serious concern about the unacceptable behavior of Ryanair staff during my sister’s recent travel experience, at Manchester Airport.My sister, who lives in Italy and does not speak English fluently, was subjected to what appeared to be discriminatory and disrespectful treatment by members of your staff. Terrible service.The flight from Sofia to Copenhagen was delayed by just under three hours due to a fault with the airline’s plane. While waiting at Sofia Airport, I received a message on my phone saying that a €4 refreshment coupon had been added to my boarding pass. €4 is barely enough for the cheapest coffee or a small bottle of water. On top of that, the link showing where the coupon could be used at the airport was not working.After asking around and finding places that supposedly accepted vouchers, I discovered that there was no €4 voucher on my boarding pass at all. I tried at two or three places, and it didn’t work anywhere. I felt stupid and misled.When boarding finally started, instead of apologising to passengers who had been waiting for 2.5 hours, the staff began arrogantly charging €75 to people whose suitcases were sticking slightly out of the metal frame. The problem is that if you have a soft bag, it doesn’t stay in the frame properly because there is no front. Very “clever”.On the plane, the crew were extremely rude and arrogant. I had to ask several times for an update on the arrival time, as I had a train connection on the other end. The stewards were clearly annoyed and said they couldn’t know what would happen in the air. No time update was given during the flight at all.Because of the delay and the late arrival after midnight, I missed my train and lost my tickets. It was impossible to travel until nearly 4:00 in the morning, meaning I spent 3.5 hours wandering around at night.When I tried to claim my train expenses based on my passenger rights through their website, the application would not submit. There is clearly something wrong with the expense claim form.Terrible.  Absolutely terrible.
Honestly the worst company I’ve ever interacted with. They make a business out of providing you the worst service and most obvious scamming techniques possible.

On 31 October 2025, I travelled on Ryanair flight FR3683 from Malaga to Lisbon with Priority Boarding, which includes one cabin bag and one small personal item.At the gate, a staff member refused to let me take my cabin bag onboard, claiming it “wouldn’t fit,” even though it was within the size limits (55x40x20 cm). She spoke rudely and forced me to pay €50, saying the bag would go in the aircraft hold.However, no one collected the bag. It was tagged as a “Cabin Manual Bag,” and I carried it onboard myself — it fit perfectly in the overhead locker. The cabin crew member Esther confirmed that the charge was unnecessary.I contacted Ryanair, but they ignored these facts and sent a generic response about baggage policy.I was charged twice for the same item and the paid service was never provided.If this issue is not resolved, I will escalate the case to the European Consumer Centre (ECC-Net) and consider reporting it to the Civil Aviation Authority (CAA) for unfair commercial practice.This situation shows poor customer service and disregard for EU consumer rights.

This is one of the worst airlines I've ever flown with. The prices are very cheap, but at the gate they become exorbitant because you're more incompetent than you can imagine. EasyGet is a thousand times better; their prices are more reasonable and consistent.

I experienced a distressing incident at Eindhoven Airport involving Ryanair staff. My flight FR5682 to Stansted Airport London was scheduled for 21:00 but departed at 21:32. I had all necessary documentation and was boarding when the flight was delayed by 30 minutes.The female staff member, whom I will refer to as Viggo, was unprofessional and arrogant. She refused to allow me to board despite my valid ticket and boarding pass. I was subjected to mental trauma and anxiety as she denied me boarding and threatened to involve security or the police.I later discovered that Viggo had targeted me to obtain a replacement ticket, which she intended to sell for additional profit. I lodged a complaint on the Eindhoven Airport website as advised by immigration staff. However, I did not receive any support and felt the process was unprofessional.Ryanair provided no assistance at the airport and the service desk was unhelpful. I was forced to purchase the tickets and pay a significant amount. In the middle of the night, I was left without transportation or services. The staff members, including Viggo, laughed at me and continued to speak in Dutch.In the morning, I presented my documentation, but Ryanair demanded payment for the ticket. As I had missed the flight, I was required to purchase a replacement ticket for €375 to return home. This resulted in a sleepless night, a lack of food and considerable physical stress.I strongly advise caution regarding Viggo and the Ryanair flight . I found myself in a difficult and financially strained situation. I hope that the female staff member will be disciplined and that her actions will serve as a warning to others who misuse their power. Ryanair services so complicated I called the number multiple times but no response and get chance to speak anyone. No service available at airport overnight and day .

We were charged 170 EUR extra for 2 people on a Lisbon–Paris flight due to Ryanair’s own operational failures.Yes, we missed online check-in by a few minutes and paid the airport check-in fee which was 110 EUR. Not ok but fine.But during airport check-in, the Ryanair employee clearly saw that we had paid for a checked bag, watched us holding the suitcase, and did not warn us that it had to be dropped off. It was a kabin size bag so we didnt think it had to be checked in and she got our money and didnt check in our bag!At the gate, we were stopped and charged another 60 EUR for a mistake caused entirely by Ryanair staff.This is not “low-cost flying.” This is profit through poor communication and passenger trapping.If you fly Ryanair, triple-check everything — their staff won’t help you, and you’ll pay for their silence.

Absolutely shocking experience with Ryanair.My husband was involved in a life-threatening car accident and was medically declared unfit to fly. We contacted Ryanair in advance, provided evidence, and asked for a reasonable solution. Instead, we were met with nothing but copy-and-paste responses hiding behind “policy”.They claim to “empathise”, but there is clearly zero empathy or human understanding when it actually matters. Even in a situation like this — a serious accident completely outside of our control — they refused to offer even the most basic gesture such as travel credit.Their only suggestion? Pay extortionate fees to change the flight (which are higher than the original ticket) — despite the fact we have no idea when recovery will allow us to travel. Completely unrealistic and unreasonable.This isn’t about wanting a refund for a change of plans — this is about how a company treats people during genuinely difficult, life-changing situations.Disappointing, inflexible, and completely lacking in customer care. I will not be using Ryanair again.

Statred travel with few passing hits from service staff while sitting in my sit, the flight was redirected and transport was supposed to be organised but a soon as we left the plane ryanair just left us with no futher information. Absolutely appalled by Ryanair. Utter contempt for their customers, verging on criminal. My family and I, along with 35 other passengers flying to Newcastle were left stranded in Málaga airport. But most importantly, they actually delayed the flights for them, they made some kind of effort to help them out, to take account of their situation.This ended up with nearly 40 people at the Ryanair desk, many of them with infants and young kids, trying but failing to receive some kind of compensation. If Ryanair staff turned up wearing masks and carrying swag bags, at least they’d be honest about their intentions. As it stands, they manage to achieve the same effect without the costume. I remain utterly baffled—truly, scientifically confused—about how this organisation continues to exist, let alone turn a profit, unless it runs on a steady diet of passenger misery and loose change shaken from unsuspecting travellers.I made the grave mistake of booking a flight to Spain for February 2026. Unfortunately, due to a significant medical issue, I can no longer fly. Cue my second mistake: not reading the terms and conditions closely enough to discover that Ryanair has a no cancellation policy. No cancellations. None. Zero. The sort of rule you'd expect from a pop-up scam website that triggers three “Are you sure?” warnings from your browser before it lets you proceed.Still, I attempted to speak to one of their representatives—or “Borg units,” as I now affectionately call them. Fully assimilated into the corporate hive mind, they could do nothing but drone policy lines at me while offering an apology so shallow it could evaporate in sunlight.I should have known better. In my personal experience, Ryanair remains the gold standard of low-morals, high-charges aviation: a penny-pinching, passenger-herding, pound-shop-tea-and-coffee-dispensing enterprise operating somewhere at the edge of the galaxy where customer service goes to die.And don’t be seduced by the cheap headline fare. Once you’ve checked your bags, declared your pockets, and possibly registered your dental fillings as additional weight, they will charge you more than the cost of the flight itself. Probably more than the plane is worth, frankly.Unless Ryanair rethink their no refund policy (unlikely) From this moment forth, I shall avoid Ryanair with the same determination as a two-year-old presented with a plate of broccoli.

Abuse against passengers of every kind. Five years ago*, I decided not to spend another euro of my money on this horrible airline. Very poor customer service from alicante Airport in Spain to Bristol. The hidden and misleading reap off on passengers is rediculous. In this case that should have been €15.60 fee not €52.I contacted Ryan Air customer support on ticket number 71377133 for more information on why the wrong fee was applied and how we can go around resolving the situation including the fact of badgering and bullying my 17 year old daughter.The customer service ticket was just closed, with no information or justification or any attempt at coming to any kind of resolution.They did not clarify how they justify the wrong fee being charged or any attempt to discuss any wrong doing at all.Given the planes are dirty internally and in poor maintenance. Don’t even deserve one star but can’t give them any less, have flown with them twice and on both occasions they have changed flights, changed aircraft, and moved our pre paid seats so we are no longer sat together and argue they don’t need to refund the paid seating because they reserve the right to change seats for operational reasons, disgrace of a company, zero customer service, will never use them again and encourage anyone else to avoid them, they aren’t worth the hassle and stress they cause, lost a day of our holiday as they cancelled the flight and we had to fly back a day early ! Over the years I’ve experienced the worse treatment possible from this airline. My recent one was last year in October I was coming back from Warsaw-Modlin to Dublin. My flight was at around 22:00h. It kept on being delayed due to fog. At 01:00h it was cancelled and we were told by Ryanair staff to leave the gates and get out to the terminal as there was bus waiting for us to take us to the hotel. No bus, no hotel. Four people got hotel as they started being aggressive. Next flight was scheduled for 09:30h. We waited and then it was delayed again by over 10hrs. We only got €4 vouchers and water was even more expensive at the airport. Once we would try to sit somewhere overnight, we would be told by airports staff that we can’t do that. All night we sat on the floor, close to the door. Ryanair customer service was sending us to airports information and vice versa. I’m still waiting for compensation from flight that happened a year ago. Another experience was that the flight was cancelled and another one was two hours later (same airport, same route). My one was cancelled for one week later and the one that was in two hours flew out. No compensation at all with over 3 hours of delay. They just cancel the flights to avoid costs while putting us in a situation hard to get out. They tell us to book hotels in the range of the airport, but there’s none available. Horrible customer service. When it comes to backpacks it’s just finding people to get more wages for making others pay for 1cm too big luggage/backpack.
Worst airline and most abusive customer treatment

I flew with my family (2 adults and a 12-year-old) from Krakow to Malta. We opted for random seat allocation. Unfortunately, Ryanair deliberately separated us, placing each family member in a different part of the plane.What made this unacceptable was that once we boarded, we realized the flight was far from full – there were plenty of empty seats.Safety contradiction: At the gate, staff specifically checked if my 12-year-old was accompanied by an adult. Yet, the seating algorithm separated us completely.Lack of empathy: On board, I pointed out the empty seats next to me and asked the cabin crew if my daughter could move so we could sit together. The request was flatly refused.There was no operational reason for this refusal, as the seats remained empty during the flight. It felt like a calculated move to prioritize revenue over common sense and child welfare. While I understand budget airline policies, enforcing them so rigidly when the cabin is half-empty is just greed. Disappointing experience.

Flight from Birmingham delayed for 2 hours and upon arrival at Dublin airport we were charged 34 euros to pick up the hire car late! We booked the hire car through ryanair! So picking it up late and using it less cost the extra 34 euros which was the same cost as 3 days car hire!!!! Diabolical!!

I am absolutely appalled by my recent experience with Ryanair and feel compelled to warn others.Our flight was changed from Leeds Bradford Airport to Manchester at short notice, which caused a huge amount of disruption and inconvenience. What should have been a straightforward journey turned into a stressful and unnecessarily complicated ordeal, requiring additional travel, time, and expense just to reach a completely different airport.To make matters even worse, the flight was then delayed by an astonishing 11 hours. Based on this experience, I would not recommend flying with them.

Review for Airport Check-In Staff – Palanga Airport (Flight to London Stansted, 27 October 2025)We recently purchased an additional ticket for our child and carefully followed the airline’s guidance on how to link it to the accompanying adult’s booking. We were advised to go to the check-in desk at the airport to ensure our seats would be together and to receive all boarding passes.Unfortunately, our experience at the check-in desk at Palanga Airport on 27 October 2025, for our flight to London Stansted, was extremely disappointing. The staff member assisting us (whose name was not visible, as her nameplate was covered) was extraordinarily rude and unprofessional from the start. She made several inappropriate comments, including “What a mess,” and “Couldn’t you just have bought all the tickets together?” — which was not possible due to the airline’s own booking system.We fully understand that ticketing issues can take time to resolve, and we would have had no issue waiting the 45 minutes it took — had the process not been made unnecessarily stressful by her attitude. To make matters worse, she incorrectly claimed that the flight was full, when there were clearly empty seats nearby once we boarded. We had even agreed in advance that our older child could sit directly in front of or behind us if needed.Travelling with two young children is already a challenging experience, but this staff member’s behavior made an already stressful situation far worse. We hope that management will address this incident seriously and ensure better training in professionalism and customer service for front-line staff.

1 star, because 0 wasn't possibleExtremely poor baggage check-in experience with Ryanair at Fiumicino AirportOur recent experience during check-in at Rome Fiumicino Airport was one of the worst we have ever encountered while travelling.We arrived at the airport to check in two suitcases and a snowboard for a Ryanair flight. Instead of a smooth process, we were required to wait more than 30 minutes in line just to access the self-service kiosks. Several of these machines were not functioning (marked with red crosses), while only one staffed counter was open. Meanwhile, two Ryanair employees at the baggage drop-off stood aside chatting and did not offer any help.When we reached the kiosk, an error appeared during the baggage check-in process. Unsure how to proceed, we asked for assistance, but were repeatedly told by staff to handle it ourselves, with dismissive comments such as “it’s easy” and “the system explains everything.” It soon became clear that many other passengers were facing similar technical issues and were becoming increasingly anxious about missing their flights.Eventually, one staff member agreed to help, though clearly unwillingly. At the same time, another employee instructed the long queue behind us to move to the service counters instead — creating even more confusion. After finally receiving assistance, we were told to join the back of that same long queue, putting us dangerously close to missing our flight.The entire process was painfully slow, chaotic, and extremely stressful. What made the situation worse was the staff’s attitude: unhelpful, unprofessional, lacking empathy, and seemingly indifferent — if not amused — by the distress of passengers.What adds to the disappointment is that the ticket prices for this flight were already relatively high compared to other flights departing at the same time. Given that pricing, we expected at least a basic level of organisation and customer support at the airport.To be fair, once we were finally on board, the flight itself was smooth and perfectly fine. Unfortunately, the ground experience at Fiumicino overshadowed everything else and left us with a very negative overall impression.

My Ryanair flight was moved by more than five hours. Under EU regulations, this meant I was entitled to a refund. I requested that the £259.94 be refunded to my bank account.Instead, Ryanair refunded the money to my Ryanair account. When I tried to transfer it back to my bank via their refund button, I repeatedly encountered a “technical error” that conveniently prevented the money from being used on anything other than another flight.I contacted Ryanair more than a dozen times to resolve the issue. I was given repeated promises they would sort it—each one different, each one unfulfilled. Then, they changed their story entirely and claimed they had already refunded £276 to my bank account. A complete lie.I complained again. Their new position? That I wasn't entitled to a refund in the first place.This farce continued for three months, at which point I escalated the issue to Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR). That, too, proved to be a waste of time. After I submitted my complaint, nothing happened for four months. It was only when I complained that there was suddenly movement in my claim. They now said they had to give Ryanair 28 days to respond... even though they’d had four months already.Eventually, I had to directly threaten legal action. Only then did Ryanair refund the money—quietly, without contact, apology, or acknowledgment. The ADR claimed Ryanair had requested a further extension to prepare a response, even though Ryanair had already refunded the money days earlier. 2 days ago I took a flight departing from Nurnberg destination to Palermo. Due to weather conditions in Palermo the plane made a go around in Palermo and diverted to Rome. We were just sitting in the plane for close to 2 hours and finally after refueling we made another attempt to land in Palermo. We had to make an another go around and diverted back to Rome. By this time we were in the airplane for close to 6 hours. After waiting in the airplane for one hour they told us we can leave here if want but they can not promise us lodging for the night (it was 22:00 local) or we can stay in the airplane and possibly attempt another landing in Trapani instead of Palermo and if we choose to stay we would have to pay for the tickets to Trapani.During this entire time, the communication was very delayed to nonexistent also so far no water/food was provided. I chose to leave the airplane with some of the other passengers. We went to the the terminal in Rome. In the terminal the RyanAir employees was very rude and dismissive the lack of communication ensued finally around midnight (8 hours after the departure from Nurnberg) we were provided with water. 3 o'clock in the morning we were transferred to a dirty, run down hotel only to be transferred back to the airport an hour later. 5 o'clock in the morning we were informed that they will try to put us in a flight taking off to Palermo in 2 hours. Due to their ''system issues'' they could not provide us with tickets and we missed that flight. Fast forward to 8 o'clock we have been told we might be able to put on the flight tomorrow but would have to pay for the ticket. In the meantime the staff that was suppose to arrange the accommodation for the day disappeared. Around 8 in the morning (19 hours after the initial departure from Nurnberg) we were told that we would have to wait 2-3 days for the flight to Palermo. At this point I decided to buy my own flights for the next day and pay for the hotel (at this point I was awake for 24+ and I wanted this nightmare to end. Also, I simply could not afford to wait another 2 days for a flight to my final destination.How this whole process was handled was a complete disaster and a nightmare. RyanAir showed absolutely no care for the passengers. During this entire time they were very dismissive and rude. I guess having passengers ''give up'' in this type of scenario is part of their business plan to minimize the costs and maximize the profit.I strongly urge anyone who is planning to fly with RyanAir to reconsider their decision. If there is an alternative go with the alternative. I am in the loss of words. They are just evil company who prays on the passengers that does not know their rights. I am sure what they did was not legal and certainly it was just plain wrong.I wanted share my experience and warn others. I also wanted to see if anyone went through a similar experience. I would highly appreciate any advice you might have for me. Can I seek reimbursement for my expense for the hotel and flight? From my research, my understanding is that I can. But please correct me if I am wrong.Rant complete. Thank you.

To Whom It May Concern,I am writing to formally raise a complaint and request compensation for a serious incident that occurred at Birmingham International Airport while attempting to board my Ryanair flight to Palma de Mallorca.I had checked in successfully and passed through airport security without any issues — both staff and systems accepted my passport as valid. However, when boarding the aircraft, a Ryanair staff member at the gate (an Indian gentleman) handled my passport roughly. In the process, the identification page was torn. Following this, I was informed that I could no longer board the flight because my passport was now considered “damaged.”This damage occurred entirely due to the Ryanair staff member’s actions. My passport was in acceptable condition when checked and cleared at both the check-in desk and security. As a direct result of this incident, I was denied boarding and suffered significant financial losses, including:The cost of the return flightPrepaid hotel and shuttle bookingLuggage fees (both outbound and return)Airport parking and fuel expensesAdditional waiting time at the airport (over one hour just to be escorted out)The total loss amounts to approximately £800–£900. I have attached supporting documentation including receipts, booking confirmations, and photos of the passport damage for your review.For context, I have used the same passport recently on a British Airways flight without issue. This clearly demonstrates that the damage caused by Ryanair staff was the sole reason for my denied boarding.Under UK261 / EU261 regulations and the UK Package Travel Regulations 2018 (if applicable), I am requesting full reimbursement of my losses and appropriate compensation for denied boarding through no fault of my own.Please provide a written response within 14 days confirming how Ryanair intends to resolve this matter and arrange repayment.I expect a fair resolution and a formal apology for the distress and financial impact caused by your staff’s handling error.

On a recent flight with Ryanair (Edinburgh to Barcelona), I experienced unacceptable and intimidating behaviour from cabin crew.Throughout the flight, crew at the rear were loudly talking and shouting across each other, creating a disruptive environment. At the end of the flight, when I approached the toilet, three crew members were standing in front of it. I politely asked them to lower their voices. Instead of handling this professionally, they surrounded me as a group, raised their voices, and accused me of lying.One crew member then locked the toilet and refused access. I was also threatened that police would be called, which felt like an attempt to intimidate rather than manage the situation.Upon arrival, police boarded the aircraft based on the crew’s version of events. Thankfully, multiple passengers who witnessed everything stepped in, challenged the crew’s account, and supported me. The officers acknowledged this and apologised. At no point was I aggressive or non-compliant.Passengers have the right to be treated with professionalism and not be intimidated or falsely escalated to law enforcement over a simple interaction.This was not a minor service issue, it was a serious failure in how a passenger was handled.

Ryanair Review – Gatwick to Nuremberg, 29 January (Scheduled 06:15, Departed 06:38)I had a very frustrating experience with Ryanair on my early morning flight from Gatwick to Nuremberg on 29th January.I arrived at Gatwick around 3:00am, parked with Purple Parking, and went straight into the terminal. We had plenty of time before departure. We sat at a coffee shop, had breakfast, and continuously monitored the departure screens for gate information.I have screenshots from 5:00am and again around 5:25am showing that no gate information was displayed for our flight. Around 5:30am, I briefly went to use the toilet. When I returned, the gate had finally appeared on the screen.We immediately headed to the gate, which took approximately 9 minutes to walk to. We arrived at around 5:51am — just 6 minutes after the gate closing time — and were denied boarding.What makes this even more frustrating is that the flight itself didn’t depart until 06:38. Despite this, the gate had already been closed and we were not allowed to board.Given that the gate information only appeared at around 5:30am, passengers were effectively given about 15 minutes to notice the gate announcement, walk there, and board — which feels unreasonable at a large airport like Gatwick.To make matters worse, the staff at the gate were dismissive and made a sarcastic comment saying, “It’s an airline, not a taxi where you can be late.” This felt unprofessional and insensitive given the circumstances.As a result, we had to purchase new tickets to Cologne later that day at an additional cost of £100 per passenger.Overall, this was a very disappointing experience. Earlier and clearer gate information could have prevented this situation entirely. Based on this experience, I will seriously reconsider flying with Ryanair again.

Do they have any care whatsoever about their customers? They do not, what an abhorrent way of conducting business.I purchased flights costing around €500 for my mother and brother to travel for Christmas. My father passed away on the 11th of December, and the flight is scheduled for the 26th of December. Due to his death, neither passenger is able to travel as we have to deal with funeral arrangements even over Christmas. I contacted Ryanair in advance, clearly explaining the situation IN ADVANCE that this was not a missed flight or a no-show.Despite this, Ryanair refused to offer any flexibility whatsoever, citing a rigid 10-day bereavement rule. What makes this especially horrible for me is that I was not even asking strictly for a cash refund. I asked whether the money could be returned in any other form, a voucher, flight credit, or a waiver of change fees whatever would work to reduce the financial loss during an already devastating time. They could even release the flights and sell them again, as they have a week! The complete lack of understanding was shocking. Grief does not end after an arbitrary number of days (10), and the emotional, practical, and financial consequences of losing a parent extend well beyond a policy cut-off. At the same time as dealing with funeral and family expenses, being told that €500 is simply lost added significant stress to an already overwhelming situation.I also made it clear that I was notifying them ahead of time, meaning the seats could still be released and resold. This was ignored.I was met with template responses and policy citations, with no acknowledgment of the human reality behind the request. This experience showed me that Ryanair’s customer service offers no compassion, even in the case of the death of an immediate family member.I would strongly caution anyone booking with Ryanair to understand that, regardless of circumstances, you should not expect empathy or flexibility when something genuinely serious happens in your life. Stay away from this horrible, horrible company.

Considering the walking distance from the main terminal area (including duty free) is approximately 15 minutes, combined with the heavy congestion at that time, it was physically unrealistic to arrive before the gate closed.The situation was not unique to us. We were a family of seven, and there were multiple other passengers and families who faced exactly the same issue and were also denied boarding. This clearly indicates a failure in the boarding and gate management process, not individual passenger negligence.To make matters worse, there were no final boarding call announcements made, and the staff on duty were unhelpful and dismissive when approached for assistance. This lack of communication and support only added to an already chaotic and stressful situation.Frankly, this experience reflects a complete disregard for passengers. Expecting travellers to navigate a busy airport and reach a distant gate within such a short timeframe is unreasonable and unacceptable.As a result of this incident, I can confidently state that I will not be choosing to fly with Ryanair again in the future. The way this situation was handled has completely eroded my trust in your service.I expect this matter to be taken seriously. I request appropriate compensation or, at the very least, a meaningful goodwill gesture such as a full refund or fee-free rebooking. They had sent one bus and told us to remain seated as they would only be unloading the plane from the front. There was a mad rush for the door as everyone wanted to be on that bus. We were informed we could not walk across the road to the arrivals area, yet the bus was gone for minutes at a time. After getting on this final bus along side the flight crew and driving along, we noticed other aircrafts had 4 + busses for passengers and the plane parkrd 2 spaces to our left, passengers were walking across the road to the arrivals area. Simply put, after all the changes made by Ryanair they didn't inform the bus company, it was not the bus companies fault. To say the driver was annoyed was an understatement. He questioned if they knew how many passengers they had flying in on the plane. On the day of our departure, guess what? The plane had been changed yet again, and our booked and paid-for seats were reallocated to other passengers who wanted them.The cabin crew on the flight was the Whizzair crew, another one of their cheap flight companies. Sept 14 2025: My First Ever Online Rant (Thanks Ryanair)I am not really one to complain. This is actually my first online rant ever. But Ryanair drained the joy from my little family holiday so completely that I felt morally obligated to whinge on the internet. Their customer service is so absent I am not even sure it was ever invented. How are they still in business? Witchcraft maybe. Cheap glue holding the planes together perhaps.We had no choice but to book with the drivel of the skies because they were the only airline flying from our local airport. The flight out to Krakow was fine. Nice, on time. We almost thought “Hey, maybe Ryanair is not so bad after all.” Oh sweet naive past us.The return journey was less flight home and more post-apocalyptic escape scene where nobody had any idea what was happening. A plane at Krakow skidded off the runway a few hours before our flight. Thankfully no one was hurt. These things happen. What also happens apparently is Ryanair immediately entering witness protection and refusing to communicate with a single human being.The information desk was chaos. Picture hundreds of passengers pleading for scraps of news while the Ryanair staff shouted at them, then slammed the shutters closed like a dodgy kebab shop. Helpful.So we headed back into town, paid for another hotel night, and got one lonely text telling us our flight had been rescheduled to 7am. Fair enough we thought at least we know. Except then at 2:45am another text arrived. Actually it is at 7pm. Or maybe 7am. Or maybe whenever the moon is in Aquarius. Who the F!@£ knows.Cue us rebooking taxis, cancelling taxis, and paying more taxis. I think we spent more on cabs than on the actual flights. The app did not work. The staff were nowhere. One Ryanair rep appeared briefly, grunted at us, then vanished like a hungover goblin.Meanwhile people were sleeping on the floor with no food vouchers and no guidance. Hundreds of us were playing passenger detective trying to figure out if we would ever see home again. Other airlines were taking off, clearing delays, and moving passengers. I'm sure they were all waving at us out the window as they flew away, Ryanair was still delayed with no explanation. (Classic)After about 26 hours in airport limbo we finally got news of a charter plane. Hooray. Except naturally Ryanair delayed that too. At this point I had aged ten years and was considering applying for Polish citizenship.Eventually almost 30 hours later we got home. I thought surely customer service will sort something out. Maybe an apology. Maybe a credit note. Even just a polite email saying sorry. Nope. Just their portal of doom, a chatbot powered by pure evil, and a wall of silence. No telephone number, no support, no humans anywhere. So here I am, ranting online like a man possessed.Ryanair, if you read this, it is not me, it is you. All you had to do was give us some info, maybe treat your passengers like actual humans, and you would have turned a zero out of ten into a solid seven. Instead, you dream of charging people for the toilets, cram us into shiny tin cans, and counting your pennies like a cartoon villain with absolutely no customer service. Shame on you!!!!Final verdict: zero out of ten. I would rather walk to Krakow next time.

My recent flight from Manchester to Tangier with Ryanair was one of the worst travel experiences I’ve ever had.	•	Terrible Seat Allocation: Despite plenty of seats being available, the booking system deliberately splits passengers who are travelling together unless you pay extra to sit next to each other. This feels like a money-making scam rather than fair seat allocation.	•	Uncomfortable Seating: The seats are extremely cramped and uncomfortable, making a 4-hour journey miserable.	•	Rude, Unhelpful Staff: The cabin crew were unfriendly and at times downright rude. Basic politeness and customer care were completely missing.	•	Shocking Food & Drink Options: There were no vegetarian options, no croissants, and no cheese toasties available—even though we ordered via the app immediately after boarding. On a 4-hour flight, all they had were alcoholic drinks, soft drinks, hot drinks, crisps, and chocolate. Completely unacceptable for a flight of this length.	•	Dodgy Baggage Scales: Their baggage scales weighed my bag 1kg heavier than the airport scales, which feels like a blatant attempt to charge passengers extra. This practice should be investigated and made illegal.Overall, this was a truly dreadful experience from start to finish. Ryanair clearly prioritises squeezing money out of customers over basic comfort or service. I will never fly with them again and can only hope this business model fails sooner rather than later.

"Be very careful! This airline extorts passengers at the boarding gate with unfair baggage fees. My backpack was deemed slightly oversized, so I attempted to discard a few personal items into an airport bin to comply with their size limits. Shockingly, the Ryanair gate staff explicitly stopped me and refused to let me remove items from my own bag. They issued an aggressive ultimatum: pay a €60 penalty immediately or be denied boarding (Cologne Germany to Porto Portugal). Denying passengers the right to discard their own property to meet baggage rules is a predatory and coercive business practice. If everything goes exactly as planned, you will travel fine. But if you have the slightest issue, get ready to hit a brick wall. Their customer service is absolutely useless: you only deal with bots that send irrelevant replies to discourage you from claiming what you are rightfully owed, even when you provide irrefutable proof.I recently had two disastrous experiences with them:1. The double-charging scam (Málaga - Tangier flight):I paid €18.49 online for a 10kg check-in bag. By mistake, I took it through security and arrived at the boarding gate. The airline logically applied its €50 gate fee to place this exact same bag in the hold. Therefore, I requested a refund of my initial €18.49, since the check-in desk service was never provided.The issue is simple and clear, backed by receipts! Yet, customer service kept sending automated responses in a loop explaining why the €50 penalty was justified, deliberately ignoring my actual request (the refund of the unused €18.49 service). They effectively charged me nearly €70 to handle a single 10kg bag and flat-out refuse to read my messages.2. Breach of European Law EU261:During a previous flight delayed for over 3 hours departing from Tangier, Ryanair left me with zero assistance. I had to buy a simple sandwich and a drink for 105 MAD (about €10). European law (EU261/2004) obliges them to reimburse these reasonable "right to care" expenses. Their response? A flat refusal, claiming that meal vouchers had been distributed. This is an absolute lie. I explicitly asked all the Ryanair staff present at the boarding gate, and they confirmed to me that absolutely no vouchers were available.Conclusion:Ryanair steals small amounts (€10, €18...) betting on the fact that customers will give up when faced with a deaf, automated, and useless customer service system. They do not read your complaints and just copy-paste irrelevant terms and conditions. I am now forced to involve the European Consumer Centre (ECC-Net) just to enforce my basic passenger rights.An absolute disgrace for an airline of this size.

Nothing like a late flight, a baggage fee for a bag that was arguably not jutting a centimeter out of the baggage measuring box and incredibly rude and uncommunicative staff to ruin a holiday experience.I use the same carry on bag with every holiday. Ryanair is not an airline. It’s a flying shakedown with wings.I’ve flown in some of the most remote places on earth, including so-called “third world” airlines, and every single one of them managed to be more professional, more honest, and more reliable than Ryanair. That alone should tell you something.This company operates like the snitches of the sky, the rats of the air, always circling to see how they can squeeze a bit more money out of passengers who don’t fly often enough to know the tricks. Every step of the journey feels designed to confuse, upsell, penalise, or quietly scam you. Luggage rules change depending on the mood, the gate, or the phase of the moon, and somehow Ryanair almost always ends up costing more than a “real” airline once the hidden extras pile up.I flew with them three times in January. All three flights were delayed by more than one hour. Not once. Not twice. Every single time. No accountability, no proper communication, just the usual shrug and silence, as if chronic delays were part of the brand experience.If you are desperate, sure, Ryanair will get you vaguely closer to your destination, eventually. If you have any alternative at all, spend the extra money and save yourself the stress, the nickel-and-diming, and the constant feeling that the airline is actively working against you.This isn’t low-cost travel. I was denied boarding by Ryanair – and what followed has been one of the worst customer service experiences I have ever had.Booking reference: GSFZPVRoute: Copenhagen – Dublin (return)I was involuntarily denied boarding at the gate despite having a valid ticket and arriving on time. The Ryanair staff clearly informed me that I would receive:• Full refund of both outbound and return flights• €250 compensation under EU Regulation 261/2004Since then, Ryanair has failed to handle the case properly.Their customer service has:– Given contradictory and incorrect information– Repeatedly ignored the actual issue– Incorrectly claimed my booking involved third-party services (it did not)– Closed or mishandled support cases without resolving anythingI have now had to reopen multiple cases just to get them to understand a very simple situation.This is a straightforward denied boarding case under EU law:Passengers are entitled to BOTH compensation (€250) AND full reimbursement of the unused ticket.Despite this, Ryanair has still not properly processed either the refund or the compensation.It should not require this level of effort to enforce basic passenger rights.I strongly advise others to be cautious — if something goes wrong, you may have to fight unnecessarily hard just to receive what you are legally entitled to.

I should have known better, but I was naïve enough to book a package holiday through Mytrip consisting of two connecting Ryanair flights. My return journey from Tenerife to Copenhagen via London Stansted took place last Saturday — on Valentine’s Day.The Ryanair flight from Tenerife was heavily delayed. When we asked the crew shortly before landing, they told us we were “only 10 minutes delayed.” That turned out to be untrue — we were in fact 55 minutes late. There were many Danish passengers on board who were supposed to catch a connecting flight to Copenhagen — also with Ryanair — and we appealed to the crew to contact their Ryanair colleagues and inform them that many passengers were delayed. They refused. “Ryanair doesn’t have connecting flights,” they said.A large group of children and adults ran as fast as we could through Stansted to reach the next flight. When we arrived at the gate — where the Copenhagen flight had not yet departed — we were met by the most rude staff imaginable. One woman was extremely cold and kept saying, “I don’t understand what you’re saying. This is very strange,” and again, “Ryanair has no connecting flights,” so what we were saying “made no sense.”There were crying children, but no care or empathy from the Ryanair staff — they were borderline mocking. We couldn’t understand why they would let a plane depart when around one third of the passengers were standing at the gate and unable to board. “That will be expensive in compensation,” we thought. But we have since come to better understand the business model. They dont compensate.We were directed to a desk where we were told that “you can get home in 4–5 days,” combined with “you might be able to get home in two days.” We were also offered the possibility — for £1,000 per person — to be placed on a waiting list for the next day, with a 50% chance of getting a seat. However, if we accepted that offer, we would no longer be entitled to the flight 2–5 days later. There was no guidance or assistance regarding accommodation in London for us or our children.I believe I may now understand Ryanair’s business model, which allows them to earn the same money two or three times:- First, they sell cheap tickets.- Because they do not take responsibility for “connecting flights,” they can disclaim all responsibility (I have heard others say they make it nearly impossible to reach your gate in time).- When the plane ends up half empty, they can resell those seats to others — for example, passengers who were stranded the previous day and are now on a waiting list.- In addition, they make money from everyone who pays £1,000 to be placed on these waiting lists.My experience with Ryanair is not — as I initially thought — simply poor customer service. It is actually anti-customer service. The customer is not the goal; the customer is the means to sell a “cheap” ticket and then earn money on that same ticket two or even three times. The final touch is speaking rudely and disrespectfully to passengers who are doing their very best to follow the rules and reach the gate on time.Our flights were supposed to cost £1,000. Instead, we spent a day and a half getting home from London — at a total cost of £2,950 for the return journey alone.It was the worst customer experience of my life. I will never, ever, ever fly with Ryanair again.My takeaway: The cheapest flights often end up being the most expensive.

An experience that can only be described as horrifying with Ryanair.This happened because of two women at the gate who, judging by their behavior, seem extremely experienced at extorting money from unfortunate passengers whose bags are just slightly over the size limit. This happened to me at Berlin airport, although I am aware that the same scam takes place at other airports where Ryanair operates.These two individuals stop you at the gate with aggressive and rude manners as soon as they sense an opportunity to charge you a penalty. In my case, it happened because I had a duffel bag that was only slightly wider than the allowed dimensions. It would have been enough to remove a sweatshirt to make it fit, but that was completely pointless.They pretend to let you try to reduce the size of the bag, making you believe you still have a chance to avoid paying the fee, but it is all just a show. Even after removing items, they do not bother to check whether the bag is actually smaller and simply reply, “It’s too big, you have to pay.”On top of that, they constantly try to rush you by saying that boarding is about to close, giving you no time to ask for explanations, even though there is clearly still time. Their behavior is disgusting—more like a mafia-style operation than customer service.I was therefore forced to pay €60 (three times the price I paid for the ticket). They only accepted payment by physical card or cash, which is quite suspicious. I doubt the transaction was even properly recorded for tax purposes.If you are unlucky enough to deal with these people, cross yourself and be prepared.

1 Star – Completely unacceptable and misleading serviceI booked an airport transfer for 2pm on purpose, knowing full well that passport control can take a long time, learning our lessons from travel the previous monht.                    Despite this, the driver arrived early, informed us they would only wait one hour, and then drove off, leaving us stranded.If the driver had arrived at the actual booked time of 2pm, as confirmed in my booking, everything would have been absolutely fine. It is not our fault that it took nearly 2 hours to get through passport control, something that is widely known to be an issue across European airports right now.I have booking confirmation and message evidence clearly showing that Ryanair/your transfer provider changed the pickup time without my consent or consultation. This was not requested by us and was never agreed to. It was not communicated to us until we were waiting in passport control and then driver messaged. To make matters worse, customer service has been shocking. It feels like a scam designed to avoid responsibility.This experience caused unnecessary stress, expense, and anxiety. I would strongly advise others not to rely on this service, as you may be abandoned when you need it most especially those solo travellers or people who have late night flights.

I will never travel with your company again. Not only will I personally avoid using your airline, but I will also warn people around me not to travel with your company. I have already convinced several people not to use your airline. Because of the hardship and distress you caused, your company has completely lost its credibility in my eyes.Why? Let me explain.On 16.05.2026, we had a flight from Weeze Airport to Sarajevo Airport. Even though we arrived at the airport on time, we were not allowed to board and the aircraft departed without us. On the same day, my pregnant daughter had arrived in Sarajevo from Turkey and was waiting for us there. However, because of your company, we could not reach Sarajevo. This caused serious distress for my pregnant daughter. At the same time, my spouse and I also experienced significant hardship in Weeze.That night, we were forced to stay at a hotel in Weeze. The next day, we had to travel to Cologne Bonn Airport and purchase new tickets from another airline in order to finally reunite with our daughter. Because of your company, my spouse and I experienced one of the worst 24 hours of our lives.When we contacted your company regarding this issue, the response we received was completely unacceptable. We were told that the problem was caused by the airport and that we should contact the airport authorities ourselves. However, once a passenger purchases a ticket from your airline, all responsibilities related to the journey belong to the airline company. It is your company’s duty to investigate passenger complaints, determine the reasons for disruptions, and take the necessary measures to prevent similar incidents from happening again. Shifting this responsibility onto the passenger is both wrong and irresponsible.As a result of this situation, we suffered the following financial losses:- €69 for the hotel- €135 for transportation/car expenses- €344 for new flight ticketsIn total, we suffered a financial loss of €548.Unfortunately, this was not the end of our problems.On 23.05.2026, we had our return flight. Your aircraft, scheduled to depart from Sarajevo to Weeze at 15:15, experienced a technical malfunction. Passengers were kept waiting inside the aircraft for approximately 85 minutes. Eventually, we were informed that the aircraft would not be able to fly due to the technical issue. Technical problems can happen, and I understand that. However, what happened afterward was extremely disappointing.After passengers were taken back to the waiting area, people were hungry and had to buy food at their own expense. Many passengers spent approximately €30–35 per person at airport cafés and restaurants. People later submitted their receipts to your company hoping for reimbursement, but almost nobody received a positive response. Hours later, passengers were given only a small bottle of water and a small chocolate cake. This treatment was completely inadequate and disrespectful toward passengers.My spouse and I also had to spend an additional €69 on food, despite choosing the cheapest options available.For all these reasons, your company has not only lost a customer but has also gained someone with extremely negative opinions about your company.

Please check your cabin bag measurements carefully before flying with Ryanair.We have flown with the same cabin bags on Ryanair flights for years without any issues, but on this trip the enforcement of the bag size policy was extremely strict. Every passenger was required to place their bag into the sizing frame, and although my bag fitted almost completely, there was only around half an inch protruding at the top. The same bag was accepted on previous flights with no problem at all.What was most disappointing was the charge — £75 per bag, meaning my wife and I were charged a total of £150 at the gate. There was no flexibility, no understanding, and no customer-focused approach whatsoever. It felt less about customer service and more about generating additional revenue from passengers.Going on holiday should be an enjoyable and stress-free experience, but this situation left us frustrated before we had even boarded the aircraft. Extremely stressful verification process and poor booking accessI had a very frustrating experience with Ryanair’s verification process after booking through a third-party provider.I completed the Standard Verification as requested, but the process only asked for my phone number and did not allow me to update the booking email address. I was then told that updates would be sent to the original booking email, which appears to belong to the third-party agent and is not accessible to me as the passenger.This creates a very unreasonable situation: I am the passenger, I have completed the verification, but I still cannot properly access or manage my own booking or complete check-in with confidence.The live chat agent told me that the verification would be completed by the following morning, but this kind of “guarantee” is not very reassuring when the system itself remains unclear and the passenger has no direct control over the booking email.The most frustrating part is that once Standard Verification is selected, Ryanair does not allow passengers to switch to Express Verification, even if the flight is approaching and the passenger urgently needs access to the booking. This feels like a poorly designed process that transfers stress and risk onto customers.A passenger should not be trapped between a third-party booking email, a pending verification process, and limited support from live chat. Ryanair should provide a clear way for verified passengers to immediately move the booking into their own myRyanair account and update the email address.Overall, this has been an unnecessarily stressful and customer-unfriendly experience. I would strongly advise other passengers to avoid booking Ryanair through third-party platforms, and I will personally try to avoid Ryanair whenever possible in the future.

I travelled on flight FR200 from Paris (BVA) to Rome (FCO) on December 6, 2025. Upon arrival, my checked suitcase — which was brand new — was returned to me with severe structural damage. The shell was cracked and punctured, making it unusable.What was even more disappointing was the complete lack of guidance at the airport.
