I lost money on a prepaid car rental and insurance. Be aware of a very clever trap on the Ryanair website.When booking flights on ryanair.com, you are also encouraged to rent a car through third-party rental companies. The website allows you to pay for the car rental and insurance in advance, even if the rental company does not accept debit or prepaid cards at the rental desk.The website also advertises “free cancellation”, but what is not clearly explained during the booking process is that cancellation must be made at least 48 hours before the rental start time.What is also not clearly presented is that even if you fully pay for the car rental and arrive at the airport, you may still be refused the car if you do not have a credit card. Many car rental companies listed on ryanair.com do not accept debit or prepaid cards for the security deposit.Yes, this information technically exists somewhere in the booking process — but you must click several small information links, then “more info”, then “terms and conditions”. At one point I was even redirected to a 6-page PDF document just to find the important details.Let’s be honest: do most people carefully read every page of terms and conditions for every service they buy? Probably not. Car rental should be simple and transparent, but the process on ryanair.com is not.A very simple question during booking such as:“Do you have a credit card (not a debit card)?”would have saved me a lot of money and frustration.In the end, I had to cancel the car rental and insurance less than 48 hours before pickup, and I received NO REFUND — simply because I do not own a credit card. I only use a debit card because I do not need credit cards.What makes this even more frustrating is that if you visit the Ryanair car rental page directly (without buying flight tickets), there is a visible “Important Information” section mentioning the credit card requirement. However, when you add a car rental during the flight booking process, this information is much more hidden and requires several clicks to find.Ryanair could easily add filters showing which rental companies accept debit cards and which do not. Instead, customers are allowed to pay first and discover the restriction later — potentially losing the full rental and insurance payment.Please be careful when booking rental cars through Ryanair, especially if you do not own a credit card.Safe travels to everyone.

The only real way to avoid Ryanair charges now seems to be either booking Aer Lingus and accepting the extra €20–€80, or making absolutely sure your bag is well within Ryanair’s dimensions.I’ve used the same bag for years on Ryanair flights with no issues. Recently, I was stopped because the lower pockets of my fabric suitcase slightly exceeded the 20cm depth — even though the top of the case was within limits. My daughter’s hard suitcase was about half an inch over the 20cm depth and she was charged. I avoided a charge on my fabric case by taking items out.However, while I was paying for my daughter’s case, staff realised we were travelling as a group of four. My husband and son were already passed through. They then called my husband back — who had already passed through earlier without any issue — and charged him after rechecking his bag. The fact that he was called back to have his bag rechecked after mine passed the criteria is what annoyed me the most about the experience. I fully respect any system designed to improve passenger safety. But if this were purely about safety, every passenger’s bag would be checked consistently. Instead, it was obvious in the queue that larger bags were not stopped.It feels like the system relies on selective enforcement — so passengers continue to take the chance by not scrutinising the bag dimension, hoping they’ll get through like they did before. That way, the charges — and the revenue — keep coming. Travelling with Ryanair is a very stressful experience and this is just one part of the journey that is stressful

We arrived at Tenerife South Airport over 5 hours before our Ryanair flight back to Bournemouth was due knowing that there can be long queues to get through passport control. A Spanish security guy marked our passport stamp and let us out telling us to collect our cases from baggage area. Again no Ryanair staff to help us. We eventually found our cases and went to the growing queue at Ryanair customer service. One person there and after 10 mins the queue was the same we decided to sort things out ourselves. It was now 9pm.We spoke to a Jet2 lady in a nearby CS kiosk who told us only 1 person missed their flight as Jet2 staff walked the queue taking people out and we presume getting them through passport control. BA did the same. Why could we not check-in earlier?(Jet2 takes your cases 3 hours before the flight).Why weren't Ryanair staff getting passengers out the queue like other airlines?Why no assistance once through passport control to explain where our cases were and how to get there?Why no additional help at customer service?And lastly we all know that Europe doesn't seem to care about us Brits missing flights due to ineffective passport machines so why does Ryanair schedule 4 or 5 flights leaving within approx 20 mins of each other?We flew back to Stansted 2 days later with Jet2 and saw how organised the process could be.

The nightmare of travelling with Ryanair! What did you expect, I must ask myself. In Copenhagen , Ryanair has their own third world terminal - a long way from the other airlines. I had no problem with my step count that day. From the time of being called to the gate, we all stood in a queue for at least a half hour and finally were allowed onto the plane. The main problem is that this plane has room for maybe 180 seats - but Ryanair have squeezed two or three more rows in. That means there is no leg room at all - if you are over 160 cms, don´t do it, because you can´t sit properly in the seat . Seat is an exaggeration ,  there is absolutely no cushioning at all so - basically a park bench with a vertical back rest. Of course , you can´t tip the seat back, like in a normal seat. Everyone on the 3 and a half hour flight were complaining of back pains , when we were finally let out of our own hell. The pilot and crew spoke their own language - I speak fluently in English , Danish, with some German and Italian. None of these languages were used when informing the passengers of this and that. Awful air conditioning was causing several of the passengers to have coughing and sneezing fits. Totally drying out our eyes and noses. Add to this the young stewardess, who could not fit in between the seats, so when she walked up and down in the aisle, she bumped into the passengers in the aisle seats - EVERY TIME she went past. So no sleep possible then. Really , it is simply not possible to design a plane seat that  is worse to sit in!! Credit where credit is due though,  the bags came through very quickly with no damage.You think your getting a cheap flight and saving money, but it´s not much cheaper than the serious flight companies, and ask yourself - is it worth the trip to the chiropractor afterwards.

In March 2024, I was denied boarding on a Ryanair flight due to overbooking. I was informed at the airport that I was entitled to a full refund and statutory compensation. I was handed a single sheet of paper which literally stated that it is against Ryanair’s policy to overbook flights – yet that is exactly what happened. I was given no meaningful assistance, no proper support, and was effectively left stranded in a foreign country. Under UK/EU Regulation 261/2004, passengers who are involuntarily denied boarding are legally entitled to fixed compensation and a full refund (or rerouting), in addition to care and assistance.After significant back and forth, the final compensation and refund amount was confirmed to me in July 2024. I was told that all I needed to do was provide my bank details in order for payment to be processed. I provided those details.It is now nearly TWO YEARS since the original incident, and I still have not received my refund or compensation.I have followed up more than 20 times via email and customer service channels. Each time I am either told it is being processed, escalated, or I receive no response at all. It is completely unacceptable that a confirmed payment has not been made.I am also extremely concerned about where my bank details have been stored over this period, and why they have apparently not been used to process a payment that was formally approved months ago.Being denied boarding was stressful enough. Being stranded abroad without proper support was worse. But having to chase a confirmed refund for almost a year is beyond unreasonable.I am now seeking urgent resolution. Ryanair, please process the confirmed refund and compensation immediately. Under UK/EU261, compensation for denied boarding is not discretionary, it is a legal requirement. The prolonged failure to issue a confirmed payment is unacceptable.

Like most everybody on this site we've had to jump through so many hoops to finally get our refund.But we did finally get it, 10 months later.....!It should be noted the information herein would only apply to people who received a refund/credit option after flights were cancelled, not those only receiving a credit offer.Time line:13 Nov 2019: book flights for 7 April 2020.18 March: flights cancelled, received email from Airasia offering refund/credit options.18 March: log in to Airasia account, open chat, select refund, informed to expect refund within 3 weeks.22 March: Australia closes borders.9 April: (the end of their 3 week processing time) to 8 September, multiple attempts to contact them, all fail. Chat bot completely useless, go around in circles (it's designed that way) contact via Facebook, same same, no call centres, no email contact, again, all designed to make it almost impossible to contact anyone there.8 September: file chargeback claim with visa card. 10 October, chargeback rejected by Airasia, conveniently 2 days after the validity time of the claim, thus no possibility to have the claim re-examined by card provider.12 October: File complaint with our local consumer protection agency.12 October: Consumer protection contacts Airasia, they obviously have an email address for a real person, unlike the rest of us who have only Aisasias' useless chat bot contact.13 October: Airasia requests bank account details for refund, with the same processing time frame of 3 weeks.12 November (4 weeks later) refund received.Hallelujah...will wonders never cease...!Obviously the only thing these dolts at Airasia will listen to is threats of legal action..!If you want your rightful refund you will need to get your local consumer protection agency involved.May all your (and our) negative derogatory incantations and bad karma be directed to Tony Fernandez and all others down the line involved in their decision to delay and resist their obligations.Quite obviously honesty is in very short supply within Airasia executives/administration.

If you book with this airline, just know that the moment something goes wrong, you are on your own with a chatbot.

**Subject:** Formal Complaint Against AirAsia – Unfair Treatment and Denial of RefundThis letter serves as an unequivocal and scathing expression of my unwavering condemnation of your airline, AirAsia, which I consider an institution utterly devoid of humanity and sensitivity.I demand a full refund for the tickets purchased through your airline due to unfortunate and unavoidable circumstances. As a result, our journey from Bangkok to Singapore—and our return via Thai AirAsia—became entirely impossible, precluding any opportunity for us to board your flight.In light of these dire circumstances—my wife’s grave medical condition, as confirmed by physicians both in Koh Samui and Bangkok, which necessitated her urgent medical evacuation back to Israel—we submitted a refund request due to our inability to travel.The condescending and demeaning manner in which you dismissed my refund request on medical grounds, despite the assurances purportedly provided by your policies, deserves the strongest condemnation. No reasonable person should be subjected to such treatment. Your management’s adherence to a cruel policy—an offensive and oppressive regulation that allows you to withhold the funds of a helpless passenger without delivering any service—is indefensible. I am only writing this review to try to help fellow passengers who have experienced problems with Air Asia. In April 2022 my wife's flight from Penang to KL was delayed by 4+ hours so she missed her connecting Oman Air flight back to Dubai.I had to book my wife on Emirates-she managed to get the last seat so she was back to work on time in Dubai.I knew Air Asia's reputation so did not bother contacting them-having had 2 previous flights cancelled I had spent 4 hours 'learning' their online bot chat-AVA- and managed to work out a way to convert the cancelled flights into flight credits-Air Asia refunds are deliberately impossible to achieve via any pathway in my view.So, I claimed about 1/3 of the Emirates return flight on Oman Air travel insurance and Oman Air also refunded the missed return flight so overall I lost 1000 MYR equivalent. Thank you Oman Air!I count myself lucky!Air Asia's latest 'creative' marketing strategy is to persuade you to buy tickets now for March-November 2023 e.g. get your money now to improve their company cash flow.I wonder how many Air Asia customers will buy these cheap tickets only to find the flights are subsequently cancelled/rescheduled rendering the flight to be useless in terms of catching connecting flights?I for one decline their 'kind' offer of cheap flights.I also suggest that Air Asia's constant cancelling/rescheduling of flights is a scam forcing you to book another ticket-in order to not miss your connecting flight/business meeting-whilst at the same time tying your original ticket money up in a credit; so in effect you buy two tickets to fly!My experience is that any 4pm/5pm flight out of KL to Penang is very likely to be rescheduled to 9pm and then arrive 1 hour late for.......Air Asia's infamous 'operational' reasons!Another person posted they surmise AirAsia make more money on not refunding cancelled/rescheduled flights than on the actual flights they do fly. They could well be correct given that the customer service is basically a bot called AVA that seems to be deliberately designed to be difficult to interact with and often goes around in a loop. After 4 hours 'talking' to AVA I discovered if you ask her an impossible question 3 times you get put through to a real person in a text chat.But beware, unless you are ultra polite to the operative they will simply cut the chat!Next vacation we are going to use a coach from Penang to KL and then use Grab to get to the airport.Yes it will be a 4 hour journey but so much less stress than wondering if we will miss out connecting long haul flight due to Air Asia's inability to fly to their advertised schedules.Trust the above helps.

"Nightmare Experience with AirAsia: Terrible Customer Service and Unfair Charges"I recently had the misfortune of dealing with AirAsia's customer service, and the entire experience left me thoroughly disappointed and frustrated. From start to finish, it was a series of mishaps that made me question why I ever chose to fly with them in the first place.Let's start with their so-called customer service. The hotline had me on hold for what felt like an eternity, only to be greeted by an unhelpful and indifferent agent when I finally got through. It was clear that they couldn't care less about my concerns.But the real kicker came when I discovered that I was being charged extra for luggage fees. Now, I understand that airlines have policies in place, but AirAsia took it to a whole new level. Not only did they charge an exorbitant fee for my checked baggage, but they also claimed it was over the limit.Here's the thing: I meticulously weighed my luggage before heading to the airport, ensuring it adhered to their supposed limits. However, AirAsia insisted that I had exceeded the weight allowance, slapping me with additional charges that felt more like a scam than a legitimate fee.The lack of transparency and accountability from AirAsia is appalling. It's evident that their priority is squeezing every last penny out of their customers rather than providing a satisfactory travel experience. The entire episode left a sour taste in my mouth, and I can confidently say that I will never be flying with AirAsia again.In conclusion, if you value your time, money, and sanity, I strongly advise against choosing AirAsia for your travels. The combination of terrible customer service and unjustifiable charges makes for an experience that I wouldn't wish upon my worst enemy. Save yourself the hassle and choose an airline that values its customers.

My First — and Last — Experience with AirAsia (Flight AK0118)After 20 years of flying with airlines across the globe, I never imagined that a simple, human request — to sit next to my wife — would turn into the worst customer service experience I’ve ever had.We recently flew AirAsia from China to Malaysia. The outbound flight was fine. But on the return leg (AK0118, Sept 11), everything changed.Despite booking our tickets together, we were assigned completely separate seats. I politely asked to be seated beside my wife — not requesting more legroom, priority seats, or any sort of upgrade. Just to sit together.The response?“We are a cheap airline.”I even offered to pay extra — but that too was dismissed without a second thought.It wasn’t just disappointing. It was dehumanizing.Being told “we are cheap” — as if that justifies rudeness, inflexibility, and lack of basic courtesy — is something I’ll never forget.I had no choice but to board and endure the flight apart from my wife, feeling frustrated, unheard, and honestly, embarrassed for the airline. I’ve flown budget airlines before — and never felt so disregarded.I learned something important that day:💥 No matter the price, respect costs nothing.Sadly, I can’t get that flight back — but I can share my story, and I will — so that others don’t make the same mistake I did.Never again, AirAsia.And to others considering it: Think twice.

A Disappointing Experience with AIR ASIA - The Worst AirlineFlying with Air Asia has been, without a doubt, one of the most frustrating and unpleasant travel experiences I have ever had.1. Booking and Customer Service The booking process was a nightmare. Their website was riddled with glitches. It took multiple attempts to complete a simple reservation, and each time an error occurred, it was difficult to determine what had gone wrong. When I finally resorted to calling their customer service, I was met with long hold times. Once I got through to an agent, they seemed ill - informed and unhelpful. They were unable to answer basic questions about baggage allowances and seat selection, which were not clearly stated on their website either.2. On - Board Experience The aircraft itself was in a sorry state. The seats were uncomfortable and cramped, with very little legroom. The upholstery was worn and had visible stains in several places. The in - flight entertainment system was malfunctioning on my flight. The touchscreen was unresponsive, and the selection of movies and shows was extremely limited compared to other airlines.3. Food and Beverage There is not served food during in flight, they sell the food during your flight. During the flight there are announcements on speakers and sell goods as well as food like some cheap street vendors.4. Flight Delays and Communication This airline has a reputation for flight delays, and my experience was no exception. Our flight was delayed by over three hours, and the communication from the airline about the delay was almost non - existent. There were no clear announcements about the reason for the delay or any estimated departure time. Passengers were left in the dark, waiting around aimlessly in the terminal.In conclusion, I would not recommend AIR ASIA to anyone. Their lack of attention to detail, poor customer service, and sub - standard on - board experience make them, in my opinion, the worst airline in the industry.

My experience with AirAsia has been extremely disappointing and unnecessarily stressful. I booked tickets from Perth–Kuala Lumpur–Colombo, but due to a genuine medical condition I was unable to travel. What followed was a long, frustrating, and completely avoidable ordeal caused by AirAsia’s repeated delays and inconsistent handling of my case.First, AirAsia delayed the medical‑related request for weeks. Then, because their own flight was delayed, they offered a free cancellation option — which I accepted and submitted. Instead of processing it, they deleted my request without explanation.After that, they asked me again to resubmit all medical documents, even though weeks had already passed. Then, after almost a month, I finally received a message saying my case was approved and they only needed a selfie for verification. I submitted it immediately and waited again for weeks with no update.I contacted AirAsia multiple times, but received no meaningful response.Then, unbelievably, I was asked yet again to resubmit the same medical documents by an AirAsia officer (JHKIM, Customer Happiness, AirAsia MOVE) — even though the case had already been approved earlier.This cycle of delays, repeated document requests, and lack of communication shows extremely poor customer service and a complete disregard for passengers dealing with medical hardship. A process that should have taken days has dragged on for months, with no resolution.Overall, this has been one of the most unprofessional customer‑service experiences I have ever encountered.

On 13 December 2024, I booked a flight with Air Asia for from Denpasar (Bali) to Perth WA, scheduled for 24 April 2024. Unfortunately, upon arrival at the airport, my client was informed that his visa was not confirmed in time for the flight. To our astonishment, no one from Air Asia had informed us during the booking process that a visa was required for this particular flight. Had we been made aware of this requirement; we would have taken the necessary steps to obtain the visa well in advance. However, this crucial information was not provided, leading to a significant inconvenience and financial loss for both myself and my client.When we sought assistance from Air Asia staff at the airport, we were met with dismissiveness and were simply told that we should have arranged the visa earlier. This response was not only unhelpful but also insensitive to our predicament, especially considering that my client does not speak English fluently, making communication and problem-solving even more challenging.I am deeply disappointed by the lack of transparency regarding visa requirements and the inadequate support provided when issues arose. Air Asia has a responsibility to ensure that customers are well-informed about all necessary travel documents and to offer appropriate assistance when faced with unforeseen circumstances.Therefore, I requested a full refund of the ticket fare for the aforementioned flight and they only return $50.00.

I have never in my life encountered a company that treats paying customers with such utter contempt. My recent flight with AirAsia from Hong Kong to Kuala Lumpur was not just disappointing — it was a masterclass in incompetence, arrogance, and corporate greed.Let’s start with the delay. The flight, scheduled for 6:15 PM, did not leave the tarmac until 8:33 PM. That’s almost two and a half hours late. They did mention that the flight was delayed by 30 minutes, but it turned into a 2 hour+ long ordeal.But unbelievably, that wasn’t even the low point. The real disgrace came during check-in. Our carry-on bag was perhaps 1kg over the limit, and instead of handling it professionally, the AirAsia staff member decided to turn it into a petty power trip.When we said we’d simply remove a few items to meet the requirement, this employee threatened us three separate times — yes, three — saying, “You can do that, but if any of my colleagues catch you, they WILL fine you HKD 1,000 per kilo.”The tone was spiteful, smug, and downright demeaning. It was like being spoken to by a petty tyrant rather than a customer service representative. We weren’t breaking the law — we were passengers trying to comply. Instead, we were treated like criminals.Let me be clear: I understand airlines need to enforce baggage policies. But there is a chasm between professionalism and predation — and AirAsia is firmly on the wrong side of it. This was not customer service; this was harassment thinly disguised as policy enforcement.To make matters worse, we had even paid for the Value Pack, which includes perks like pre-selected seats, onboard meals, and a 20kg checked baggage allowance. This wasn’t a “free flight.” We paid, we followed the rules, and we were still publicly humiliated and threatened for a trivial, fixable issue.AirAsia’s entire business model appears to be built on nickel-and-diming passengers while stripping away every ounce of dignity and decency. It’s one thing to be a low-cost airline — it’s another to operate with low-cost ethics.After this ordeal, I can say with absolute certainty: I will never step foot on an AirAsia flight again. I would rather pay double, even triple, the price on another airline than endure another second of this degrading nonsense.AirAsia should be ashamed — not just for their incompetence, but for the way they make their customers feel: belittled, powerless, and disrespected. This airline is the definition of cheap in every possible sense of the word.Avoid AirAsia at all costs. No amount of savings is worth the humiliation.

Air Asia is cheap and efficient, but with very limited leg room for taller people. But I don't think they have thought very carefully about the safety issues related to their Hot Seat policy. Because so called hot seats, which include all the front rows and the emergency exit rows are more expensive, they are often empty. I've been on flights with nobody in the exit rows (and even worse, the staff sometimes put the tray tables down during the flight to prevent people sitting in the emergency seats... In an emergency that would delay anyone getting to the exit). In any emergency the fact that no-one is there to open the emergency door quickly means there would be a serious delay (every second counts!) to getting out of the plane if you sit near the middle of the plane. Also, I've often seen people who would obviously be incapable of opening the emergency doors, which need to also be picked up and thrown out in the event of an emergency. A friend of mine who is 82 was sitting in one on one flight. But no Air Asia staff have the courage to do what is right and ask such people to move. The international safety regulators should look into this! Instead of leaving empty, why not just upgrade suitable people who pay for other seats and offer them an emergency exit seat. That would help ensure quick opening of exits in an emergency. I wonder if there was anyone in those emergency exit seats when the Air Asia flight crashed in Indonesia. In that case it probably wouldn't have made a difference, but in other cases it might!  Finally, AirAsia is still using plastic cutlery in an age of awareness over Single-use plastic. Why can't they have reusable utensils....

My flight with AirAsiaX itself went smoothly, but what happened after landing was absolutely unacceptable.When my brother and I arrived in Kuala Lumpur, the immigration machine tore his passport. As a result, he was denied entry to Malaysia — which we completely understood. However, since he couldn’t enter the country, he was placed under the responsibility of AirAsiaX, and that’s when everything turned into a nightmare.My brother was detained for three days in what they called a “waiting room,” but it was more like a cell: over 70 people crammed into a small, overheated room (around 30°C), sleeping and eating on the floor, with no Wi-Fi and very limited access to toilets.The AirAsia security staff (wearing AirAsia badges) were extremely rude, verbally aggressive, and intimidating. Only two of them showed any respect or basic humanity. It was impossible to ask questions or get any information.Meanwhile, the embassy specifically asked me to stay in Kuala Lumpur to follow the situation and not leave my brother alone. This forced me to extend my stay, pay for several extra hotel nights, buy new flight tickets, and cover many transportation costs — including countless Uber rides between the embassy and the airport where my brother was being held.Thanks to our embassy’s efforts, a laissez-passer was eventually obtained so that he could return home. But even then, AirAsiaX continued to make things worse: they refused to transfer him from one terminal to another (from T2 to T1), refused to handle his luggage, and delayed the process so much that he missed his flight. We had to buy new tickets at our own expense and spend an additional night at the airport — all because of their negligence and lack of organization.This experience was traumatic, inhumane, and completely unacceptable.I have traveled extensively around the world and have never seen an airline treat passengers this way — especially one that claims to be the “World’s Best Low-Cost Airline.”We lost money, time, and peace of mind.AirAsiaX’s behavior was disgraceful and deserves to be investigated.I have photo evidence supporting everything I’ve stated here.Avoid AirAsiaX at all costs. Subject: Formal Complaint – Inadequate Meal Voucher Option for Vegetarians (Flight AK-643 Delay)Dear AirAsia Customer Service,I am writing to formally raise a concern regarding my recent experience with Flight AK-643, which was originally scheduled to board at 11:20 a.m. but was delayed to 3:05 p.m.—a delay of nearly four hours. During this long waiting period at the airport, my family and I, including my elderly mother, were understandably in need of refreshments and food.After inquiring at the service counter about any assistance for food and water due to the delay, we were eventually provided with meal vouchers. However, we were disappointed to find that the voucher was restricted only to Burger King.As a family of strict vegetarians, who also avoid eggs, onion, and garlic due to religious reasons, Burger King was not a suitable option for us. We respectfully requested either:An alternative voucher valid for another outlet in the airport offering vegetarian food that aligns with our dietary needs, orA cash equivalent, orA note to the inflight crew to ensure we could be provided with a vegetarian meal onboard.Unfortunately, all our requests were denied. The staff at the counter were firm that only Burger King could be used and refused to consider our dietary restrictions. As a result, we were left without food for the entire duration of the delay, which caused unnecessary distress and discomfort.We understand delays can happen, but we kindly urge you to consider the diverse dietary needs of your passengers in such cases. Offering a single non-vegetarian restaurant as the only option is not inclusive or fair to passengers with dietary restrictions.We hope AirAsia will take this complaint seriously and ensure better alternatives are provided in the future—either by:1. Offering more flexible food voucher options,2. Allowing passengers to choose from a variety of outlets, or3. Providing cash equivalents when necessary.We would appreciate a response and an explanation on how AirAsia plans to address this issue going forward.Sincerely,[Harrinee A/P Kalimuthu ][Flight Number: AK-643 | 04 /08/25]

My experiences with Air Asia have been VERY bad. It's easy to book online, but fixing issues or making changes is difficult or sometimes impossible. While the apparent cost is low, baggage is expensive add-on, also "transaction" fees. It's necessary to sign up as a member to do anything more than book. Then you can sometimes make simple changes, like time or date - but only at least 48 hours before the scheduled flight, and also doesn't work for flights with layovers, and you can't change destination. Changing a flight means losing any purchased add-on, you have to buy your baggage allowance, etc. again, and there are more additional fees. Anything more than very simple time / date changes requires talking (actually text chatting, as there is apparently no voice line) with an agent. But first you must get through the horrible less than worthless "Thou Shall NOT Pass" bot (previous female avatar "AVA", now male avatar "Bo"), which only offers otherwise available "help" articles and platitudes. For example, it will say something like You want to cancel a flight for credit? OK, what is your booking number? Then its like, Just kidding, you can't cancel your flight, only we can do that. But first it offers a cryptic message "Please interact with a card or click Cancel", and continues to reply with that message no matter what is entered, though eventually you might get through. Sometimes after arguing with the bot for upwards of an hour it will finally say "...I transfer you to my fellow Allstar". Then, you are in a queue, usually a few hundred. The queue tends to go down fairly quickly (my experience so far), just keep the web page open and it continues to update. So MAYBE in an hour or few you are FINALLY connected to a human agent. Long delays between responses, like they are working on many cases at the same time. Agent may or may not be helpful, if fixing an Air Asia caused problem (for example they rescheduled my layover flight to arrive before leaving) they can maybe do that. But changing a flight within 48 hours, changing a destination, etc. They just say "no" and hang up abruptly. Even at the physical Air Asia counter at the airport, questions like "I'm unexpectedly early, can I get an earlier flight or have to wait many hours for originally schedule flight?" the answer is a rigid "NO", even if they have open seats on an earlier flight. Because of my very bad experiences with Air Asia, resulting in losing many hours and thousands of Bhat over a few incidents, I avoid using them.

Avoid, avoid, avoid!! Honestly, the worst experience with dealing with an airlines, that I have ever had in my life. My two friends and I booked 3 flights with Air Asia as it came up as the cheapest option on our routes. What are the odds of one airlines messing up all three flights? Well, somehow AirAsia managed to do exactly that! Two flights they cancelled before our travels commenced. This meant we had to rebook our flights with a different airline at a much higher late as it was closer to the travel date. Although their website says refunds are processed with 14 days...more than 2.5 months after AirAsia cancelled their flights, we are still waiting for a refund. They are taking this long to refund us for flights that THEY cancelled. I have followed up multiple times with no success. (Booking ID KB1GYE and XZGZ4F) The third flight was moved earlier about 4 hours, which was frustrating as we had to get up at 4:30am to be able to get to the airport on time. It was annoying but we did it because we were just grateful that they didn’t cancel this one. At the airport it was absolute chaos, they had one single queue for all of their flights, so we stood in line for about 2 hours for check-in. It was getting close to the time that our flight check-in time was going to close, so we proactively approached the ‘last call’ desk to let them know the situation and request that we be able to check-in. The Air Asia staff at the counter told us we couldn’t yet as our flight hadn’t been announced for last call and to return in 20 minutes. So, we waited for 20 minutes as she had told us to and returned to the last call desk to check-in after that. Now their staff told us that we were too late to check-in and we couldn’t board the plane?!!! After telling us 20 minutes ago that it was too early for last call check-in and to wait for 20 minutes, they suddenly changed their story and said we had missed last call check-in and were unable to board. The first staff member was clearly untrained and had given us incorrect information, her superiors apologised for her miscommunication but still would not let us board our flight. They just told us to write a complaint on the website (Booking ID K3YDGZ) AirAsia took zero responsibility for the mess that they caused. We had to book our flight with another airlines for 3x the price of our original flight plus bear the cost of the missed Air Asia flight. We also had to wait at the airport for 4 hours and miss the activities we had planned for that day. Just absolutely horrendous experience all around. Avoid at all costs.

However, it turned out that I had bought a bottle of water at the airport and therefore the weight exceeded the allowed limit by 200 grams. I offered to leave the bottle, but they said that it was too late now and I would have to pay 2500 pesos. I asked how it was calculated, they could not answer and they would not give me a receipt for the amount, which makes me suspect that the management of Air Asia does not know anything about the method, but that it was invented by creative employees. If you want to fly with Air Asia, they may offer the smallest seat space in Asia, the worst service, the only airline where the cabin crew is downright hostile and where they cancel flights without providing any form of compensation.

I have just had the worst flying experience I have ever had. I’ve been traveling through Asia with just a backpack and a camera, and I’ve already flown with AirAsia twice before. On both of those flights, nobody caused any issues about my modest bags — until today, June 16.First, the flight was delayed by almost two hours. And because the Vietnamese airport doesn’t accept e-boarding passes, I had to go to the check-in counter just to get a paper one. I didn’t even have any baggage to check, like on my previous flights with this airline.But the woman at the counter refused to give me my boarding pass and insisted on weighing all my bags — my backpack and my camera bag — which together weighed about 10–11 kg. She said she wouldn’t allow me to board unless I paid 55 euros extra.I was shocked. I only had 50 euros left to survive the next two days until I got home. I told her that I had flown with the exact same bags before with no problem and that I just wanted to go home. I explained that I couldn’t afford the fee and even showed her my bank account. Still, she refused.I asked her to call her supervisor. He told me the same nonsense and completely ignored the fact that I had previously flown with the same baggage twice. I filmed everything and will share it with full details on social media.Later, airport security got involved. I told them I didn’t want any more problems — I just wanted to get home. They told me to either throw my belongings away or give them to someone else (which makes no sense at all when you’re a tourist).Eventually, I threw away my second pair of shoes and removed some items from my bag until it weighed exactly 7 kg. I had to trash the rest. So instead of just letting me return home, they caused unnecessary stress and financial burden.And all this — for a 50-euro ticket — they demanded another 55 euros on top. This is honestly one of the worst experiences a passenger can have. I strongly recommend everyone stay away from this airline. I will make a video about it and expose how they treat passengers so poorly over trivial matters. I’m still in shock.I will contact their customer service and also post everything on social media. I just hope they don’t give me another problem during boarding. I’m still waiting…

My husband and I recently had a frustrating experience flying with AirAsia while traveling from Cairns to Nepal. We originally booked an AirAsia flight from Cairns to Bali, departing on April 12, and our next flight from Bali to Nepal via Malaysia was scheduled for April 14 with Batik Airlines.At the Cairns airport, we received our boarding passes for the flight to Bali, but when it came time to board, AirAsia staff informed us that we could not proceed because our transit time exceeded 24 hours. As a result, we were unable to board the flight. To adjust our plans, we booked another AirAsia flight from Cairns to Bali for April 14. However, upon arriving at the airport, we were again told that unless our connecting flight from Bali to Malaysia was also booked with AirAsia, we would not be permitted to board. Despite paying extra for baggage, we were further informed that we were not allowed to bring it on the flight.Because of this misleading information, we had no choice but to purchase an expensive emergency ticket from Bali to Malaysia and apply for an emergency visa, resulting in unnecessary financial stress. Upon arriving in Bali, we realized that no such airline policy existed requiring passengers to book consecutive flights with the same airline. The incorrect guidance led to unnecessary expenses and mental strain.To add to the frustration, we attempted to contact AirAsia for a refund via their provided support email, only to receive a notification stating that the email could not be delivered. As a result, we have been unable to resolve the issue or recover our funds, further compounding the distress caused by this experience.This situation highlights severe miscommunication and poor customer service, leading to undue financial and emotional hardship. I urge AirAsia to ensure their staff provides accurate information to prevent passengers from facing similar inconveniences.

Used Air Asia a lot prior to pandemic but had a recent bad experience with them. The flights themselves are ok but if you have a problem and need to contact them then this is where your problems start. I was trying to book a flight to travel internally in the Philippines. After one airline changed the flight times leaving me no choice other than to cancel, I tried Air Asia. I noticed on their site that you can now book other Airlines via Air Asia but wondering why you would do that and not just go to the other Airline direct I proceeded to book what I thought was an Air Asia Flight. Turns out it was a Philippine Airlines flight that I had just booked. I received an e-mail advising me to wait up to 24 hours for flight confirmation. When I did not receive that I waited ages to connect to AVA which seems the only way to contact them. I was eventually told that my schedule would be e-mailed to me which it was so I thought all was complete. Next day I received an e-mail from Air Asia saying that they have not been able to confirm the flight and have started the refund process. More frustrating waits to connect to AVA and then having one operator cut me off and eventually they stated it was too late to do anything and refund process already started. This was at the end of March and I was flying two weeks later. I had no choice other than to wait for refund and only had my money back two weeks ago at time of writing 3rd July. I booked the same Philippine Airlines flight that Air Asia were not able to confirm with Philippine airlines direct so there were plenty of spaces on the flights and it meant I had to pay for the same flight twice. It was only when I was checking in to one of the flights in the Philippines that I was told I was double booked on the flight so it seems Air Asia did actually book it but their process to confirm the flights with a rival airline was just not functioning. Word of warning - if you want to book a flight with Air Asia for a non-Air Asia airline then just save yourself the pain and don't do it. Their AVA system is next to useless. If you wait for the operator to speak once connected and they do not reply then the conversation times-out and you have to wait again. The only way to stop the time out is to keep the conversation active by typing something. Worst customer service experience ever!

Judging by the most recent communication from Air Asia, their customer service team has been rebranded as “Customer Happiness”, a term more appropriate for a branch of McDonalds than a professional airline company. Nevertheless, given this company’s tendency to write to people as if they are five-year-olds, it is an appropriate title. And given the behaviour of an Air Asia crew on a recent flight, they shouldn’t simply use a “McDonalds” term – but go and work there.I have been travelling with Air Asia for over twenty years; I’m not intolerant, nor am I nit-picking, I accept that travel does not always go smoothly and, above all, I accept that planes experience turbulence to the extent that you become so familiar with the ‘patterns’ of turbulence that they go by almost unnoticed.For the first time for me – and I have been on hundreds of flights – this event was markedly different and, without exaggeration, I genuinely thought the plane was in difficulties. The plane shook – violently – from side to side on at least two occasions. The anxiety amongst the passengers was palpable, a deep silence that, to me, conveyed genuine fear. And, indeed, the deep silence from the crew was far from comforting: is this plane in such difficulties that the pilot is too occupied to talk to us?What warnings or explanation or apologies or, in particular, reassurance were heard from the pilot? You’ve guessed it: none. That is an arrogant and dismissive attitude on the part of the air crew. That their air stewardess looked at me like a rabbit in headlights when I expressed my annoyance upon disembarking – without a word to say in reply – is far from a “happy” customer service.I lodged a complaint, only the second I have ever lodged in two decades flying with this company: no marks for service but full marks for a patronising and superficial response. Here are the edited highlights, their remarks and my responses[i] "I understand how unsettling turbulence can be" With respect, whether or not you consider it to be euphemistically "unsettling" is immaterial and, frankly, such a patronising response is unwelcome. For the first time since I began using Air Asia over 20 years ago, I considered it to be sufficiently terrifying - a more appropriate term in my view - to warrant communication. [ii] "the turbulence you encountered during landing was indeed related to weather conditions" I am perfectly aware of that and I did not question the cause of it; if I had believed it to be aliens I would have said so.[iii] "we strive to provide clear communication during such events"Your company was founded thirty years ago and yet you are still "striving" to provide a protocol on an aeroplane? Are you suggesting that such a protocol is not established, that there is no unequivocal instruction pertaining to a pilot's communication with passengers in such events? You state that you will forward this concern "for further review". What is there to review? A plane was shaken violently from side to side on at least two occasions, no warning was given, and not a word was said to the passengers, despite which it is presented to me as something your team might think about.[iv] While it may be the case that weather conditions are unpredictable - I have been on more than enough aircraft to appreciate that - it still does not detract from the fact that nothing was said to the passengers and there is no indication from you that the pilot was questioned and / or advised pertaining to that specific point. If the event did not warrant an altitude change then the least the pilot could have done - mid-flight - was [a] apologise and [b] reassure the passengers that the turbulence was not extraordinary and would be short-lived because I did regard it as extremely unusual. [v] "I appreciate your understanding and patience as we continue to work on improving our services."No, I am neither patient nor understanding with regard to this. This was a cause of severe distress on the part of an experienced aeroplane passenger. That you continue to work on improving your services is nothing more than a superficial "get-out clause" with neither admission of culpability nor offer of compensation.Air Asia: a company where children send “happiness” emails to people they think are children.They might be children – but we’re not.If that happens again, I shall sue this company to screaming point.

Summary: Run, walk, crawl, or swim to your destination, and AWAY from Air Asia. The biggest scam in the world. Detailed breakdown: My wife and I had a ONE-way booked from Bali, Indonesia to Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia. My wife ended up buying some gifts from the Ubud market and we had some extra weight we threw into an extra bag. I tried to pay for extra luggage online, over the phone, numerous times, but no luck. Either you're on hold forever or the online chatbot is useless. After 3-4 times of trying a few days in a row, I said forget it, our flight is in a day or so, we'll just check in the extra bag at the counter and pay for it. We get to the counter the day of our flight and the lady says "you have an extra bag" I say "yes I'd like to pay for it as extra luggage." She says $600 USD. I said WHAT?!!??!?! The whole flight for the both of us didn't cast a fraction of that. It's a one-way 1-hour flight. Her logic was because I didn't do it online, it's much more expensive in person. I explained to her that I truly tried, but their online system sucks and doesn't work. She says it's not her problem and if I want to take that bag I have to pay $600. I obviously told her there's no way in hell I'm paying $600 for one bag one way, that's highway robbery. I asked to speak with a supervisor or someone who can use common sense. She refused and said her supervisor will tell me the same exact thing. I told her to ask....how do you know his response if you didn't even ask him or let me talk to him. So she makes a phone call and then says "supervisor says he'll make a one time exception and he's never done this before and he can get in trouble for doing this, bla bla bla" So I was grateful and said okay, we're getting there....until she hits me with "he'll reduce it from $600 to $550" LOL!!!!!! I said there's no way in hell I'm paying that. She says well, your gate closes in 10 mins, so either you pay it or you miss your flight. I was actually down to miss my flight but my wife was feeling very sick that day. It would have been cheaper to just rebook the flight and ship the back straight to the US. So I paid the damn $550 and will NEVER EVER fly Air Asia, as a matter of fact, I truly hope either:1) they TRULY get their act together under new ownership or management.    2) They shut down. I prefer the latter. At this point, you're thinking well just call your credit card company and dispute the charge. They already thought of that, they made me sign a fat agreement form when I made my payment....so they used that as proof when I disputed the charge. My CC company said hey that's your signature with their terms and price disclosed that you agreed to. They corner you into paying outrageous prices in desperate situations. Horrible business practices; this isn't a good business this is the devil's work.

Never again Air Asia!We travelled from Frankfurt via Dubai to Singapore to start our Malaysia tour there.We also had a total of five domestic flights to Kuching, Mulu, Kota Kinabalu, Kuala Lumpur and finally to the island of Langkawi.We flew internationally with Emirates and the domestic flights mainly with Air Malaysia - very pleasant airlines.We only flew the last domestic flight from Penang to the island of Langkawi with Air Asia.When I arrived in Langkawi, I discovered that Air Asia had managed to damage my one-year-old suitcase so badly that I had to throw it away.The whole corner of the suitcase was torn off! How they manage to do something like that will remain a mystery to us.We reported the damage on site at the Air Asia counter.In the email exchange with Air Asia, we were offered 80 Malaysian Ringgit (MYR) as compensation. That's the equivalent of 17.10 euros for a suitcase that cost 150 euros a year ago!After we complained about this, we were finally offered 100 Malaysian Ringgit, with the comment that Air Asia's liability was limited and the compensation offered was final and binding!The compensation offered to us was increased from 17.10 euros to a whopping 21.38 euros!!With all the effort required, we politely declined! We don't need alms!!1) A photocopy of your Passport2) A photocopy of your Bank Account (preferably Bank Passbook or Bank Statement)3) Bank Swift Code (International Transaction)4) Authorization Letter (for 3rd party account - if necessary)We will never travel with Air Asia again!We have also informed our travel agent about this, and they will also distance themselves from Air Asia and no longer recommend Air Asia flights to customers.--- deutsche Übersetzung ---Nie wieder Air Asia!Wir sind von Frankfurt über Dubai nach Singapur gereist, um dort unsere Malaysia Rundreise zu starten.Wir hatten insgesamt auch fünf Inlandsflüge nach Kuching, Mulu, Kota Kinabalu, Kuala Lumpur und schließlich auf die Insel Langkawi.International sind wir mit Emirates geflogen und die Inlandsflüge hauptsächlich mit Air Malaysia - sehr angenehme Fluggesellschaften.Nur den letzten Inlandsflug von Penang auf die Insel Langkawi sind wir mit Air Asia geflogen.In Langkawi angekommen, musste ich feststellen, dass es Air Asia geschafft hat, meinen ein Jahr alten Koffer so zu beschädigen, dass ich ihn entsorgen konnte.Die ganze Ecke des Koffers war abgerissen! Wie man so etwas schafft, wird uns ein Rätsel bleiben.Wir meldeten den Schaden noch vor Ort am Schalter der Air Asia.In dem E-Mail Verkehr mit Air Asia bot man uns nun 80 Malaysische Ringgit (MYR) als Entschädigung an. Das sind umgerechnet 17,10 Euro für einen Koffer, der vor einem Jahr 150 Euro gekostet hat!Nachdem wir uns darüber beschwert haben, bot man uns zuletzt 100 Malaysische Ringgit an, mit der Anmerkung, dass die Haftung von Air Asia beschränkt und die angebotene Entschädigung endgültig und verbindlich ist! Man erhöhte die uns angebotene Entschädigung von 17,10 Euro auf sage und schreibe 21,38 Euro!!Mit dem dafür noch nötigen Aufwand haben wir dankend abgelehnt! Almosen brauchen wir nicht!!1) A photocopy of your Passport2) A photocopy of your Bank Account (preferably Bank Passbook or Bank Statement)3) Bank Swift Code (International Transaction)4) Authorization Letter (for 3rd party account – if necessary)Wir werden nie wieder mit Air Asia reisen!Wir haben auch unser Reisebüro darüber informiert, auch dort wird man von Air Asia Abstand nehmen und Kunden keine Flüge mehr mit Air Asia empfehlen.

The company offers a program called the Air ASEAN Pass. For a prepayment of around $300, it claims you can purchase flights within ASEAN member countries at a very low cost. I bought it and used it for a few months, but I found it to be practically a scam. Here’s why:1. Flight Booking Restrictions: You can only book flights that are at least two weeks in advance. This means that you must plan your itinerary at least two weeks ahead of time.2. Limited Flight Availability: The number of available flights is extremely limited. Most flights can only be booked two months in advance.3. Domestic Flights Only: The discounted flights are almost exclusively domestic, rather than international within ASEAN.4. Pass Deactivation: The worst part is that after purchasing a few cheap tickets, the company disables your ASEAN Pass, meaning you can no longer purchase tickets at a discounted rate.5. Questionable Operations: Despite claiming to be a Malaysian company, their Air ASEAN service is actually operated by a Chinese company. When contacting customer service, you may be dealing with someone in China, and it’s a complete waste of time.6. Deceptive Practices: The company promotes the Air ASEAN Pass as a way to buy cheap tickets, but in reality, they impose various restrictions to ensure that you can't get good value for the $300 you've paid. Moreover, they tamper with the online booking system behind the scenes, ending the service prematurely to ensure they profit from your purchase.

The email said someone would be in touch but from experience I knew this to be untrue so I tried to contact customer service. There is no way to call or email Air Asia customer service just an automated chat feature (ava very slow and useless). I initially tried to rebook through the ava system but it didn’t work and told me I needed to speak to an agent, takes a while to do this but eventually puts you in a queue of hundreds  to chat to a human agent (average wait time was 3 hours I tried 11 times (add up the billable hours  wasted there :/ ).Now Legally I am due a full refund for the full trip as it was the airline that cancelled (voucher can be offered but so must a full refund) so after that is what I requested the return trip I originally booked  no longer existed on any date at all through AirAsia. When I eventually got through to an agent  there is instantly  language barrier (understandable it is and Asian airline) they would either waste so much time the chat timed out so I had to join the queue again from the back or all but 1 lied an said a refund wasn’t an option at all (even though you are given a list of options before getting the agent and a refund is one of them). The one that didn’t lie timed out after asking for a copy of my cancelation email (they would already have that info on the booking?). Sooo back in the queue when I eventually got through again, I was met with the most arrogant rude and dumbest agent ive ever come across (and I’ve owned call centres), he not only gave the same lies and robotic statements the others did but got abusive, at one point stated he know the airines T&Cs better that I do so I should shut up and do what he says, in response I sent him a screen shot from AirAsia website stating if they cancel they will refund and then asked him to end the chat because there was no option to do so my end again and continuing was getting me nowhere, he lied saying he couldn’t (clearly just wanted to keep the chat open to avoid work as plenty of agents had done before).   Eventually I gave in (after the chat queues started just not connecting to anyone) and ended up taking air Asia credit. I had paid in USD as all their flights were in this currency but I received credit in IDR and it worked out to be was £25 less than the amount I originally paid.     On top of this with one of the flight I originally booked no longer excising I had to book part of the trip with a different airline at more expense  (AirAisa do offer other airlines flights on their site but the prices are nearly double and you can’t use the credit for this). So not only do I not have the fights I originally booked, but I am now £300+ out of pocket, have had to change hotel reservations, the airport shuttles, covid tests etc and have wasted days of my time.  I have lodged numerous complaints but again its a brick wall of scripted responses not even related to your complaint or their favourite trick just ticking off the complaint as closed just to make you go away. I even tried the customerservices and the CEOs twitter accounts but no response from either. I did get one email apologising for the abusive agent but nothing more just some generic scripted response and a dead link.  I can not see how this company is still in business with the dubious way they run their company.Never ever use this airline if you can help it they seem desperate for money.

However, this time I booked the journey as one single ticket directly through AirAsia, and I reasonably expected that there should not have been any problem with the connection.Even if my first flight arrived late due to weather, I expected that the next flight would either wait for me or that your team would provide proper assistance, because the delay was clearly not my fault. You should also have had the information from your side that I had already checked in for the flight from Kuala Lumpur to Perth, and that the reason for my late arrival was the weather delay.What makes this even more disappointing is that someone named Alexia Leong Gan Hung sent an unbelievable email stating: “Without Prejudice / Remarks: Not Applicable.”What does this even mean?It is extremely disappointing to receive such a response, especially from someone holding the title Head of Customer Happiness, without any proper explanation or support.I understand that I booked with a low-cost airline, but that should not mean customers are treated without care simply because they paid for a cheaper ticket.This issue caused me to miss my next flight from Perth, and I also received a warning from my workplace because I was unable to arrive on time.Now, receiving such an unexplained and dismissive response from customer service team only makes the experience worse.If AirAsia is unable to take responsibility in situations like this, then you should stop selling single-ticket connecting flights, or at the very least provide a clear warning that such situations may occur and that no responsibility will be taken.That way, customers can be properly informed in advance and avoid putting themselves at risk in the future!!!🥺😤😔🥹😕😔☹️🙁

On December 23, 2025, on AirAsia flight D7 523 from Tokyo Haneda (HND) to Kuala Lumpur (KUL) (departing 11:55 PM, arriving around 6:50 AM), my elderly mother was injured onboard. About one hour after takeoff, while walking to the restroom in a dark cabin, the aircraft experienced a sudden, severe jolt with no turbulence announcement or warning. She fell and struck her head and upper body.Due to her condition, my mother had to be physically supported by two flight attendants back to her seat, clearly indicating she could not walk unassisted. After the fall, she became incoherent and developed visible swelling on her temple. A passenger seated directly behind my parents—a Japanese woman—noticed the commotion and asked if they were okay, recognizing that something was wrong.My father immediately requested that a medical professional onboard be located due to concern for a possible head injury. Although this request was relayed forward, no medical assessment was conducted during the flight, and at one point my mother was left unattended while my father was separated from her.After arrival in Kuala Lumpur, no wheelchair or medical assistance was arranged, and there was no medical handover or coordination with airport medical services, despite her condition. Family members who observed her pale complexion and unstable walk had to locate assistance themselves.What is most troubling is AirAsia’s response afterward. Despite numerous attempts to escalate this serious incident, management did not conduct a meaningful review and instead issued a brief response containing factually inaccurate findings, claiming that medical care and wheelchair assistance were offered and declined. This did not happen. A polite “thank you” note written by my father for a limited act of assistance—intended as courtesy and in hopes of follow-up—was later mischaracterized as evidence of satisfaction, which is misleading and inappropriate.This experience raises serious concerns about how AirAsia handles in-flight medical incidents involving elderly passengers and how such incidents are later documented when complaints are raised. It leaves a fundamental question unanswered: where are the truth and integrity when outcomes appear to protect the airline rather than its passengers?I am sharing this so other travelers, especially those flying with elderly family members, can be aware and take precautions.

Absolutely Terrible Experience – Avoid AirAsia at All Costs!This was, hands down, the worst travel experience I’ve ever had, and I wouldn’t wish it on anyone. My flight from Ahmedabad to Kuala Lumpur was initially scheduled for 23:10, which in itself was already a late flight. But did the flight depart on time? Absolutely not. It got delayed repeatedly—first to 2:15 am, then to 4:15 am, and eventually to 5:05 am. As I write this review, it’s 04:58 am, and boarding STILL hasn’t even begun! The lack of communication and regard for passengers’ time is absolutely appalling.On top of this, we booked our flight through a third-party website (like many people do these days) and weren’t provided with any information about baggage weight limits—just the bag dimensions. We packed accordingly, only to be ambushed at the check-in counter with a rule about a 7 kg total weight limit across both our bags. This fine print wasn’t mentioned anywhere on the booking website, and the staff didn’t care. They demanded an outrageous fine of 7700 INR without any room for understanding or cooperation. And no, the situation didn’t get better after speaking with the so-called “manager,” Pradeep Kulkarni. Instead, we were treated with the coldest, rudest attitude possible. Not once did the staff attempt to apologize or even listen to us.Oh, but wait—it gets worse! One employee told us we could pay 3000 INR and go, but another insisted on the full 7700 INR. Apparently, scamming customers is part of the job description at AirAsia. There was no consistency in their policies, just a blatant attempt to extort money from us. I’ve flown on countless airlines, and NEVER have I seen a laptop backpack being weighed—until now. And they weren’t even applying this ridiculous policy uniformly; they were selectively harassing certain passengers, clearly trying to make a quick buck off of us.Let’s add insult to injury: I’m 24 weeks pregnant. There I was, waiting in the check-in line, and did anyone from the staff offer me priority? Of course not! They couldn’t have cared less. No consideration for the situation, no compassion, nothing. It’s as if they went out of their way to be as unhelpful as humanly possible.Thanks to AirAsia and their utterly incompetent staff at Ahmedabad, including the unsympathetic, rude manager Pradeep Kulkarni, my babymoon started in the worst way imaginable. Save yourself the headache, the stress, and the outright robbery—DO NOT fly with AirAsia.

Scoot do not even deserve 1 star. My daughter is as I write stuck in Singapore. This was her first solo trip and is flying to Thailand from Perth with a stopover in Singapore. First of all the flight was delayed then we managed to get her onto a flight with Singapore Airlines so that she can make her connecting flight to Phuket. After just coming off a terrible 13 hour Scoot flight I’d like to caution anyone choosing this company. They call themselves a low cost long haul airline but this model really doesn’t work because the starting cost isn’t actually that low and when you factor in all your other costs you’ll likely end up paying more. There no inflight entertainment, no charging points for your own devices, no water, no blankets, no food in fact no anything unless you chose to pay extra for very poor & expensive options. We paid $50 for family ‘snacks’ and sandwiches that were disgusting. Oh and you can’t pay by debit card, or Amex. Only cash or selected credit cards.There’s no individual air blowers (so the whole plane is set to close to freezing temperature) the seats are creaking and the planes feed like there from the 80s and falling apart. Plus your long haul baggage allowance is one 20kg per family so you’ll have to squeeze everything for your two week holiday in to bags to haul on the the plane with you, or pay €120 a bag extra.Low cost, budget, crappy service works ok in Europe on short haul flights but not long haul. Yes you can download your own entertainment, load up on power packs and bring your own food (if you can sneak it through undetected) but really I wouldn’t bother.Also check carefully to see if you’re actually flying direct or have a 4 hour diversion via Athens like we had. The lower price may look attractive but I really wouldn’t bother with such a poor flying experience. Avoid Scoot at all costs.

The worst experience ever. Our flight from Berlin to Athens got cancelled just prior to checking in. The company did not take any responsibility for allocating us to different flights. This is an immoral company which buries backdoor inside terms and conditions. I have bought depart-and-return VTL tickets from Scoot. I didn't manage to take the first flight as I was not able to get my PCR report on time. What has astonished me is that Scoot has cancelled my return flight without my acknowledgement. I have called the help center for **multiple times** trying to recover back my return flight, but eventually they rejected to recover the flight, and also rejected to refund me. They blamed me that it was my fault as I didn't notify them my absence for the first flight, and it was written inside the Conditions of Carriage. Scoot is trying to take advantages of its customers by putting strange terms and conditions which are easily to be overlooked for your first read. Imagining that a customer have booked a room from a hotel to stay for multiple days and have made full payment, it is unreasonable and immoral to tell the customer that the booking will become totally void and there will be no refund when the customer fails to check in the room on the arranged date.I have officially made a refund request at Scoot website. Although it may seem stupid and may not change anything, by all means I am going to make police report and lodge a complaint to Consumers Association due to UNFAIR and UNETHICAL trade practices if I couldn't get a satisfied reply from them within 10 days.

they canceled my return flight without even telling me. they did not offer an alternative or any help at all. They just left me stranded. I had to buy another ticket at a cost of £700, had they informed me of the cancellation this would have been a lot cheaper.I claimed my entitled compensation from Scoot, to be told they had emailed me on the 7th July and informed me of the cancellation (and no it’s not in my junk folder, I checked with my private email provider) they had not made contact with me, they are basically just lying as this gets them out of having to pay compensation.I asked for proof of this and received something a 10 year old could have knocked up in photoshop, It did not prove anything. This airline is the worst!! We called in on June 26th to book luggage for our flight on July 6th. The agent we spoke to said that we didn't have to worry about it, that we each had a bag included with our tickets, in addition to our carry-ons. Bad service and all bad .. my

I usually don’t write reviews, but my recent experience with Scoot deserves to be shared. During check-in on 9 August 2025 in Manila, I requested to purchase an additional 20 kg baggage allowance for my mum, who was on the same itinerary. Unfortunately, the charge was mistakenly applied to my dad’s instead, who was in a different itinerary, and I was never informed of the total cost which was PHP 43,300 before payment was processed. I understand that excess baggage costs more when paid at the airport, but at no point was I told this would be treated as “excess baggage” or what the actual charges would be. The ground staff used vague terms like “non-refundable” and “unfathomable,” but did not provide a clear breakdown or written confirmation prior to charging my card. If I had known the full cost upfront, I obviously would not have agreed to proceed, as I could arrange a cheaper alternative. I raised this issue with Scoot customer service, but my concerns were greatly dismissed. Despite requesting escalation and a review of how the payment was handled, I received a generic response with no real acknowledgment of what went wrong or how it could have been avoided. I’m not expecting a full refund or compensation, but I do expect clear communication and accountability, especially when large sums of money are involved. It’s disappointing that Scoot wasn’t willing to offer even a small goodwill gesture or take responsibility for the lack of transparency. I hope Scoot improves its airport processes, particularly around informing customers of costs before payment is made.

When there are no unplanned events, then it can be said that the company itself is not so bad. Scoot could get a lower passing grade. Lower, because their planes, although the date of the first flight was not so long ago, only a few years, but the interior of the plane looks worn, the wings are stuck with fix tape, which somehow caused me discomfort, the staff on all my flights so far were not as friendly as on competing ones airline company. It is not possible to bring drinking water at Changi Airport (Terminal 1) and if you need a smaller amount of water to drink your medicine, you will have to pay about €3 for that.If a problem happens, then you will see how this company is solely profit oriented and how much it does not care about passenger satisfaction. Calling their outsourced call center is just a waste of time and energy, in fact, they don't even offer adequate information about the delayed flight or lead them to the wrong conclusion in order to make a decision that suits them even though the passenger is at a loss in such a situation.I can freely say that they lied to me and that is why I made wrong decisions during my last flight with them that cost me several hundred euros more than it should have been. The call center claimed that they could not provide an earlier connecting flight (I had an appointment that I had to be on time for), so I asked them to cancel that connecting flight (never get money back) and booked another flight, with another company at the highest possible price because the reservation was only a few hours before departure. Later it turned out that other passengers got the termin we requested, but directly at the airport.The ticket itself is not significantly cheaper than the serious competition that preserves reputation and has planes with far more passenger comfort equipment to be able to behave like that.Once I was forced to demand from them, due to their mistake, a refund for the flight and even after four months I have not received the money back. Communication on this is one-way with auto-responders or simple rejections without explanation and this kind of communication with them seems like a waste of time to me. The second time they delayed the flight for 2 whole days and when I demanded compensation, they just coldly refused me without any explanation, which is not in line with EU regulationsI know from my experience that I will not fly with this company again because I prefer to pay 20% more and save my free time and nerves. In fact, with hidden costs, it is no longer 20% because their flights from Berlin, where I flew from, are in the early morning (probably in order to get cheaper treatment at the airport), but this then requires me to come a day earlier in Berlin, paying for hotel accommodation, etc.

Managing my booking online did not allow me to buy more checked luggage, constantly gives error and tells me to buy my luggage at the airport. At the airport I had to pay penalty per kilo. Customer support does nothing to solve the problem. A scam, do not trust the cheap price.

They broke my luggage during a flight and told me to contact their agent (Charles Taylor Aviation) at this email (scootclaimsATctplc.com). I tried to contact them via the chat on their site, they said they would have reached out soon, still no replies. Don't trust this company

They rescheduled my sister's flight to Xi'an China by more than 2 hours later which means she'll arrive 1220am midnight on her own! The worst airline I’ve ever flown with.I was on an 11-hour flight that was treated like a low-cost short-haul. Don’t use this airline unless you want to be stranded in Bangkok.We flew from London > Bangkok with Scoot on 12th April, all was fine apart from a couple of hours delay and an email a few weeks pre departure letting us know the flight time would be moved forward by 3 hours (fine). We couldn’t access “Manage my booking” online where apparently this was change communicatedUnfortunately we weren’t the only ones in this position on the day, there were a number of customers at the desk with us waiting and in the same position.After this, we had no option to purchase a new flight with Qatar Airlines for £637!!Since being back to London we have called the airline and they are so unhelpful and basically can’t do anything (again!) which then ends in them hanging up on you.

Horrible airline. Their flight from Cebu was about 1 hour delayed, or "retimed" as they call it. So my transfer time in Singapore was cut from 90 to 30 minutes. Scoot sends me emails several times a week, about promos, their magazine, mileage stuff or a letter from their CEO. When they leave a bag behind, total silence. No info, no update no apology. I was left waiting at the conveyor belt in Jakarta like an idiot, after my last fellow passenger had left I went to the lost & found counter. The lady pretended to be helpful and assured me delivery to my hotel the next morning. Mind you, I was left without clothes, cosmetics and medicines in a city I had never been before, late in the evening. They didn't offer a t-shirt, a disposable undie or even a tooth brush. Nothing.
